This is an fMRI study of heroin-related cue reactivity using visual stimuli, with within-subject drug cue > neutral contrast and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses. The manuscript reports whole-brain voxelwise paired t-tests and whole-brain correlations (thresholded and cluster-corrected) and provides peak coordinates for significant clusters. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (heroin visual images), MRI field strength (3T Siemens Tim Trio) and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met and no exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking-related (cigarette) cues and matched neutral images, addressing cue-reactivity (I3). Within-participant Craving>Neutral contrasts are reported for each group and used in analyses (I4). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain random-effects voxelwise analyses with cluster-correction, coordinates, and t-values for Craving>Neutral and between-group contrasts (I5, I6). Methods include participant demographic information, cue type (nicotine/cigarette), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX), fulfilling I7. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of visual smoking cue-reactivity (smoking images vs. neutral) using within-subject contrasts (smoking>neutral). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (random-effects GLM and mixed-effects ANCOVA) with cluster correction and lists regional results. Participant characteristics include sex (only women), cue type (nicotine/smoking images), MRI scanner field strength (Siemens Trio 3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager QX). Thus it satisfies the review’s requirements for task-based whole-brain activation maps to visual drug cues and reports relevant methodological/participant details. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task effects, or non-empirical) apply.

Although the study presents fMRI CBF responses to cocaine-related videos (visual drug cues) and thus meets I3, it does not report a within-participant task contrast of drug cues > control stimuli nor a group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked activation map. The analyses are primarily seed-based connectivity (amygdala–dorsal cingulate) using wavelet transform coherence and individual-level ASL connectivity measures. These are connectivity-focused results rather than unmasked whole-brain task activation maps required for inclusion. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E4 (only seed-based/connectivity analyses without a whole-brain task activation map).

This study includes an fMRI drug-cue task (drug word > fixation/neutral contrasts), reports within-subject drug>control contrasts, describes participant demographics and scanner/processing details, and uses visual stimuli (words). However, the full text presents only ROI-based fMRI results (OFC and ventral striatum) and does not provide or report a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map or coordinates/figures for whole-brain activation that could be used for voxelwise meta-analysis. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain task activation reporting and meets exclusion criterion E3 (only ROI analyses reported).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cannabis-related cues (vs matched control images) during a Stimulus Response Compatibility task. The primary within-subject contrast (approach-cannabis & avoid-control > avoid-cannabis & approach-control) isolates cannabis cue-related approach bias relative to control stimuli. Analyses were conducted at the group level using whole-brain, voxelwise methods (FLAME, cluster-corrected Z>2.3, p<.05) and reported as activation clusters/figures and discussed with localization atlases. Participant age/sex, cue type (cannabis), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL/FEAT/FLAME) are all reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts of hedonic food cues > nonfood objects. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM8 with a whole-brain cluster-corrected threshold and reported MNI coordinates/figures. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are met.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, employing a within-subject contrast (alcohol > neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses are reported (cluster-corrected maps via Monte Carlo/AlphaSim) with results described in the text and figures/tables. Relevant participant information (age range, sex representation), cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5/SPM8) are clearly reported. The study does not rely solely on ROI, connectivity-only, or between-group-only contrasts. Therefore it meets all review inclusion criteria.

The paper is an fMRI study of practice effects on a sustained attention/incentive task in cocaine users, not a cue-reactivity study. It does not present analyses of drug- or natural reward-related visual cue reactivity (I3 not met) and, in this secondary analysis, monetary conditions were averaged such that there is no within-participant contrast of reward cues > control stimuli reported here (I4 not met). The study does report whole-brain, group-level voxelwise SPM analyses and provides participant demographics, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength, and processing software (meets I5, I6, I7). Exclusion is therefore due to failure to meet required inclusion criteria I3 and I4.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward (food) cues with a within-subject contrast of appetitive > neutral stimuli. Group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) random-effects GLM analyses and activation maps were reported (cluster thresholds and brain regions described). The paper reports participant sex and age matching, stimulus type (food images), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (Brain Voyager). Results are not limited to ROI, connectivity-only, or between-group-only contrasts. Therefore all inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity fMRI studies using visual natural rewards are met.

This study uses visual monetary reward cues and includes within-subject Reward>Neutral contrasts, and reports participant demographics, scanner (3T) and processing software (FSL/AFNI), satisfying I3, I4, and I7. However, the reported fMRI results are limited to a priori ROI analyses; the authors state whole-brain analyses were run but report only ROI results and do not provide group-level, voxelwise whole-brain maps or coordinate tables. Therefore it does not meet the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps (I5) and falls under exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual stimuli of natural rewards (food). It includes within-participant contrasts of appetizing (food) > control stimuli (bland foods and non-food objects). Group-level whole-brain analyses were performed and reported (whole-brain uncorrected results at P < .001 with coordinates in supplemental materials), in addition to ROI analyses. Relevant methodological details are reported: cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5); participant details are referenced and reported in participant tables/supplemental material. No exclusion criteria are met (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task activation maps).

This is an event-related fMRI study that used visual reward-predicting cues (money and cigarette images) and measured cue-elicited anticipatory activity and trial-by-trial behavioral responses. Within-subject contrasts (parametric increase in reward level; reward vs. 0-level) were modeled for both cigarette and monetary cues. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were conducted using SPM5 (3T scanner) with reported thresholds and anatomical coordinates. Participant characteristics (sample sizes, age comparability, smoking status) and cue type (nicotine) are reported. Thus the paper meets all specified inclusion criteria for a whole-brain visual cue-reactivity fMRI study and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual video stimuli in a within-subject design. The authors report within-participant contrasts of smoking videos > neutral (haircut) videos and present group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (corrected p-values and reported activated regions; coordinates/peaks described). Methods report participant age range and sex breakdown (15 smokers, 7 females, ages 18–40), cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (3 T), and processing software (fmristat, ANIMAL). The paper therefore meets all required inclusion criteria for visual cue fMRI whole-brain task activation and provides the necessary methodological details. No exclusion criteria are met (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-only, not non-empirical).

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward cue-reactivity using visual food stimuli in healthy fasting adults (n=27). The study includes a within-subject contrast of food>scrambled control images and reports group-level, whole-brain task-evoked activations (food vs control) with voxelwise results and coordinates. Relevant methodological details are provided (age, sex distribution, cue type: food, scanner field strength: 3T, processing software: SPM5). Although the primary personality correlation used small-volume ROI correction, the paper nevertheless reports whole-brain main effects and exploratory whole-brain analyses, satisfying the whole-brain evidence requirement. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (high-calorie vs low-calorie images) to probe natural reward-related cue-reactivity. It includes within-participant contrasts (HC vs LC food cues) and reports group-level whole-brain, voxelwise analyses (second-level repeated-measures ANOVAs) with cluster-level FWE correction; results and coordinates are presented (including Supplementary Table 3). Methods report participant age/sex matching, cue type (food), MRI scanner (3 T), and processing software (SPM12). Thus it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

This paper is a trial design/methods report that does not present empirical fMRI activation results or whole-brain task activation maps. Crucially, the planned cue paradigm uses personalized auditory imagery scripts (audio cues), not visual stimuli, so it fails the review's visual-stimulus requirement (I3). Although the protocol includes within-subject alcohol>neutral contrasts and reports scanner field strength and processing software, the primary planned fMRI analysis is PPI (seed-based psychophysiological interaction connectivity), and no unmasked, whole-brain task-evoked activation maps are reported or presented. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria for studies that only report seed-based/effective connectivity without whole-brain task activation (E4) and is a methods/design paper without original fMRI activation data (E6).

This is an empirical fMRI cue-reactivity study in alcohol-dependent patients using visual alcohol images and neutral control images. It includes within-participant contrasts (alcohol > neutral), reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses with coordinate peaks and corrected thresholds, and provides participant age/sex, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5/SPM8). Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This study does not meet the review’s core inclusion criteria. It used PET imaging of mu-opioid receptor binding rather than functional MRI, and it did not employ a visual cue-reactivity task or any within-participant drug-cues>control-stimuli contrast. Although voxelwise PET binding maps were analyzed, there is no whole-brain, task-evoked fMRI activation map from a cue-reactivity paradigm. Therefore criteria I3, I4, and I5 are not satisfied. The study is empirical and reports neuroimaging data, but it lacks original fMRI task activation data required for this meta-analysis.

This study is a randomized clinical trial of rTMS for smoking cessation and does not include any task-based functional MRI data or analyses of cue-elicited BOLD responses. It does not assess cue-reactivity with fMRI visual stimuli, nor does it report any within-participant drug-cue > control whole-brain contrasts or provide group-level whole-brain statistical maps or coordinates. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria for this meta-analysis (I3 and I5) and does not provide the necessary fMRI activation data.

This study does not assess drug- or natural reward-related cue reactivity using visual drug/reward cues; it examines insula reactivity to negative emotional faces (affective stimuli) in smokers. Therefore it fails inclusion criteria I3 and I4. Additionally, the paper reports ROI-based analyses (insula ROI beta extractions) and does not present a whole-brain, voxelwise group activation map for a drug cue>control or reward cue>control contrast, violating the requirement for whole-brain results and meeting exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting). Participant details are reported, but the task/stimuli are not drug/reward cues, so the study is not relevant to the review objective.

This is not an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual drug or natural reward cues. The study uses arterial spin labeling (PCASL) to measure cerebral blood flow during rest and a spatial working-memory task, not presentation of drug/food/sexual visual cues. There is no within-participant contrast of drug-cues > control stimuli or natural reward-cues > control stimuli. Although whole-brain perfusion and connectivity analyses are reported, they do not constitute task-evoked, visual cue-reactivity contrasts required by the review. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria I3 and I4 and does not provide the required whole-brain task activation maps for cue-reactivity. Exclusion criterion applied: E4 (no relevant whole-brain task activation map for cue-reactivity; study focuses on connectivity/rest-task-rest perfusion rather than cue-reactivity).

This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with an explicit within-participant contrast (alcohol vs. neutral/abstract). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed and described (SPM5; voxelwise maps used to identify ROIs), and whole-brain results are reported in the text (coordinates and cluster information). Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), MRI field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. Therefore the study meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study in chronic smokers using visual stimuli (aversive and appetitive smoking images and matched neutral images). The study reports within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control (aversive smoking-related vs neutral; appetitive smoking-related vs neutral) and group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses (one-sample t-tests) with cluster correction; results are described in text and supplementary tables/figures. Required metadata are reported: participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM5). Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking and neutral images, reporting a within-participant contrast (smoking > neutral). The authors conducted a group-level, whole-brain mixed-effects (FLAME) analysis for the smoking vs. neutral contrast (cluster-corrected p<0.05) and describe the whole-brain activation and overlap with the DMN ROI. Participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T/Siemens Trio), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This study used a monetary incentive delay task probing neural responses to monetary reward anticipation and receipt in cocaine-dependent versus control participants. It is not a cue-reactivity study presenting visual drug-related or natural reward-related cues, and it does not include within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control stimuli or natural reward-related cues > control stimuli. While the paper reports whole-brain task activation results and provides coordinates and participant details (meeting I5, I6, and I7), it fails to meet core inclusion criteria I3 and I4 for this meta-analysis (visual cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards). Therefore the study must be excluded.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study of alcohol vs control but uses gustatory (taste) cues delivered via a gustometer rather than visual stimuli. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I3 (requires visual stimuli). Although it meets other requirements (within-subject alcohol>control contrast, whole-brain voxelwise analyses, reporting of participant demographics and scanner/processing details), failure to use visual stimulus modality excludes it from the meta-analysis focused on visual cue-reactivity.

Although this is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual smoking cues with a within-subject smoking>neutral contrast and reports participant demographics and MRI details (meets I3, I4, I7), the study’s analyses are ROI-based using a priori 4mm spheres and subsequent regression models. The manuscript does not report a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map or voxelwise group activation results. Therefore it matches exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting with no whole-brain results) and fails to meet I5, so it must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of visual nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity with within-participant smoking>neutral contrasts and whole-brain random-effects results, and reports scanner field strength and processing software. However, the provided full text does not explicitly report participant age information (only that 21 women were studied). Inclusion criterion I7 requires relevant information regarding participant age and sex; age is not reported in the provided material. Because not all inclusion criteria are met, the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food images) with healthy adult participants. The study reports within-subject contrasts comparing high-caloric > low-caloric food cues (and interactions with bitter vs. water conditions). Both exploratory whole-brain voxel-wise analyses and ROI analyses are reported, with group-level statistical maps summarized in text/tables/figures and coordinates (supplementary material referenced). Relevant metadata (participant age/sex, cue type: food, 3T scanner field strength, and processing software SPM12) are provided. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study of visual natural reward (food) cue-reactivity in humans. It used visual food vs. non-food pictures with a within-subject contrast (food>non-food), conducted whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses (FLAME1) with cluster correction, and reports whole-brain results in figures/tables. Relevant methodological details are provided: participant age and sex (pre-pubertal children, 7–11 yrs), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of visual drug/natural reward cue-reactivity.

This is an fMRI study of visual natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using pictures of personally-craved and not-craved foods. It reports within-subject contrasts (e.g., Look Craved > Look Not Craved and Look Craved > Look Neutral) at the group level. Whole-brain, voxelwise task-evoked analyses are performed and reported (cluster thresholds, peak coordinates, cluster sizes) and small-volume relaxation for the ventral striatum is described. Participant demographics (ages 10–23, all female), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM12b, FSL BET, AFNI AlphaSim referenced) are all reported. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not only between-group contrasts). Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of drug/natural reward visual cue-reactivity.

This is an empirical BOLD-fMRI study using visual food cues (pictures) to probe natural reward-related cue-reactivity, with within-subject food>non-food contrasts. The authors performed group-level voxelwise whole-brain analyses comparing food-cue reactivity after glucose vs. water (FDR cluster-corrected), in addition to ROI analyses. Participant age and sex are reported (19–25 years; 12M/13F), cue type is food, scanner details (MAGNETOM Prisma; parameters consistent with 3T) and processing software (FSL) are provided. Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria for (I3) task modality and cue type, (I4) within-participant food>control contrasts, (I5) group-level whole-brain voxelwise task analysis, (I6) whole-brain results reported in text/figures, and (I7) participant and methodological details reported. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only reporting, connectivity-only analyses, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study of smokers using visual stimuli and reports within-subject contrasts (smoking>neutral, positive>neutral) with coordinates and methodological details. However, the group-level statistical tests were performed within an a priori anatomical mask (AAL regions), i.e., ROI-restricted voxelwise analysis rather than an unmasked, whole-brain group-level task activation map. Because the paper reports only ROI-masked results and no unmasked whole-brain task effect, it fails the review’s whole-brain evidence requirement and meets exclusion criterion E3.

The fMRI component used monetary reward cues (10 pence) and an incentive-conflict task rather than drug-related or the natural reward cue types specified by the review (e.g., food, sexual) — it does not assess drug or natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity. Although the study includes within-participant contrasts (reward vs nonreward) and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise results (with coordinates and thresholding), the cue type and study objective do not match the meta-analysis focus on drug and natural reward cue-reactivity. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I3 (and I7 re: relevant cue type) and must be excluded.

This is an empirical fMRI study of appetitive (natural reward: food) cue-reactivity using visual cues (pictures combined with tones) and liquid-food US. The main within-participant contrasts include CS+ > CS− (natural reward-related cues > control cues) at group level. Whole-brain group-level GLM analyses were performed (flexible factorial), and whole-brain exploratory results are reported (Supplementary) with coordinates; ROI SVC was applied for specific hypotheses but whole-brain task-evoked maps are available. Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12) are reported. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not non-empirical). Therefore the paper meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of visual food-cue reactivity in adults with obesity and includes within-subject contrasts of food > object. However, the manuscript reports ROI-based analyses (COPEs extracted from 14 a priori ROIs) and presents ROI-wise mixed-effects results; no group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map, coordinates, or whole-brain figures/tables are reported in the text or supplementary materials. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence and meets exclusion E3 (ROI-only analyses). Inclusion criteria I3 and I4 are met, but I5 (whole-brain results) is not. Additionally, scanner field strength is not clearly reported.

This study is a behavioral cue-reactivity experiment (17 non-treatment-seeking alcoholics) that measured self-reported craving and mood after alcohol vs. water cues and alcohol/placebo primes. No functional MRI data, no neuroimaging methods, and no whole-brain or voxelwise task activation maps are reported. Therefore it does not meet the core inclusion requirement for fMRI studies of cue-reactivity (I3) and related imaging criteria (I4–I7). It should be excluded from a meta-analysis restricted to fMRI cue-reactivity studies.

This study used pupillometry (eye-tracking) to measure cue-reactivity and did not acquire or report any functional MRI data, scanner field strength, or fMRI processing/software. It therefore does not meet the core inclusion criterion requiring a functional MRI study (I3) and provides no whole-brain fMRI activation maps (I5/I7). As such it is not eligible for a meta-analysis of fMRI cue-reactivity studies.

This article is a symposium/review-style paper summarizing multiple human laboratory studies of stress and alcohol using guided imagery, IV alcohol self-administration, and clinical assessments. It is not an original fMRI study of drug or natural reward cue-reactivity using visual stimuli; the cue modalities described are primarily guided-imagery/audio and mood induction, not visual cues. The paper does not present a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) fMRI activation map, nor does it report scanner field strength or processing software for whole-brain fMRI analyses. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI-visual-cue and whole-brain analysis inclusion criteria and fits the exclusion of non-empirical/summary papers lacking original fMRI data.

This study is a resting-state, seed-based functional connectivity analysis comparing alcohol dependence and internet gaming disorder. It does not include a task-based cue-reactivity paradigm using visual stimuli, does not report any within-subject contrast of drug (or natural reward) cues > control stimuli, and does not provide whole-brain task-evoked activation maps. Therefore it fails the core inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity fMRI studies and meets the exclusion criterion for studies reporting only seed-based connectivity/resting-state analyses.

This is an fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (high-calorie food images) and reports within-subject contrasts between cue conditions and passive viewing/baseline. A group-level whole-brain RFX ANOVA was performed and corrected whole-brain maps/fROIs are reported. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager) are all reported. Although neutral non-food control images are not used, the paper reports within-participant contrasts (e.g., up-regulation/suppression vs passive viewing and condition vs passive baseline) and whole-brain results, satisfying the review’s requirements for natural reward cue-reactivity with whole-brain task-evoked maps.

This study does not meet the core cue-reactivity inclusion criteria. Although it is an fMRI study with whole-brain results (and reports participant age/sex, nicotine cue type, 3T scanner, and AFNI processing), the task is a real-time smoking decision paradigm with stress manipulations and nicotine delivery rather than a visual cue-reactivity paradigm comparing drug-related visual cues versus control visual stimuli. There is no within-participant contrast of drug cues > control visual stimuli reported. Therefore inclusion criteria I3 and I4 are not met.

The study is an fMRI investigation of food (natural reward) cue reactivity using visual stimuli and reports within-participant contrasts (fattening vs nonfattening foods). However, the neuroimaging analyses are reported using an a priori ROI approach and regional summary measures (ROI averages and ICCs). The paper does not report group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or whole-brain coordinates/results; therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence (I5) and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting). Other required study descriptors (age/sex, cue type, scanner, software) are reported, but the lack of whole-brain results prevents inclusion in the meta-analysis.

The paper includes an fMRI experiment (study 1) that uses visual cues (cartoon/image of a milkshake) to elicit anticipatory responses to a natural reward (food). It reports a within-participant contrast (anticipatory milkshake cue > anticipatory tasteless-solution cue), and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses with cluster-level q-FDR correction and reported activation coordinates (postcentral gyrus). Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (Siemens Allegra 3T), and processing software (SPM12, FSL) are provided. Thus all required inclusion criteria are met.

This is an fMRI study of food cue processing (natural reward) with whole-brain group analyses and thorough reporting. However, it does not include a within-subject contrast of reward cues > control (e.g., food images versus neutral/non-food images). All experimental conditions use food pictures and contrasts are between task instructions (inhibition, imaginary eating, passive viewing) applied to the same food cues rather than food cues > control stimuli. The meta-analysis requires at least one within-participant cue>control contrast; because that is absent, the study does not meet inclusion criterion I4 and must be excluded.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food images) with within-subject contrasts (high-calorie foods>objects and low-calorie foods>objects). Group-level voxelwise analyses were conducted (they report whole-brain thresholding at P<0.001 FWE in addition to ROI small-volume corrections) and results are described in the text and figures/tables. Relevant methodological and participant details are provided (female participants, ages 19–28, food cue type, 3T scanner, SPM8 processing). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state only, includes within-subject contrasts and whole-brain task analyses). Therefore the study meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity using visual natural reward stimuli.

This is an fMRI study of smoking cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (pictures). The authors report within-subject contrasts comparing smoking versus neutral pictures (LCSP minus LCNP) that index attentional bias/cue-reactivity. Analyses are group-level whole-brain random-effects GLM results reported voxelwise (between-group comparisons and one-sample main effects) with peak coordinates and Z-values provided. Scanner field strength (3T), processing software (SPM5), participant ages and sex distribution, and cue type (nicotine/smoking) are all reported. The paper does not rely solely on ROI, connectivity-only, or non-empirical methods. Therefore it meets all listed inclusion criteria (I3–I7).

This is an fMRI study using visual drug-related film cues in amphetamine-dependent participants. It reports a within-subject contrast (Drug movies > Neutral movies) and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked results with FWE correction. Methods report SPM12 processing, 3 T scanner field strength, and participant age and sex (male, 20–65). Therefore it satisfies criteria for visual cue-related fMRI, within-participant drug>control contrast, whole-brain task activation reporting, acceptable reporting formats, and required participant/scanner/method details.

Although this study used visual food cues and within-participant contrasts (palatable vs non-palatable vs neutral) and reports scanner field strength and processing software, it does not provide usable whole-brain task-evoked results (no reported group-level whole-brain statistical maps or coordinates). The authors state that between-group voxel-wise analyses did not survive correction and that activation for each condition relative to baseline is not reported. The meta-analysis requires group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task maps or coordinates; absence of reported whole-brain results prevents inclusion.

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual Pavlovian cues (colored lights and snack images) paired with food rewards (natural rewards). The study reports within-participant contrasts of reward versus control cues (e.g., CS+ > CS− and specific PIT contrasts S1/S2 > ITI) and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise activation results (FWE-corrected) with maps deposited on NeuroVault. Participant demographics (age/sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and preprocessing/software (SPM12) are reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity to natural rewards using visual stimuli.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (cigarette) cue-reactivity that used visual stimuli (dynamic smoking videos). It reports within-subject contrasts (smoking > neutral), conducted voxelwise whole-brain analyses (with reported peak coordinates and z-statistics), and provides participant age/sex information, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes task-evoked whole-brain maps). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the review.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking cues (smoking vs neutral) and reports within-participant smoking>neutral contrasts. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed and reported (one-sample t-test and flexible factorial Time x Stimulation interaction with voxelwise thresholds and cluster extent), with results presented as clusters/figures/tables. Relevant methodological details are provided: participant age/sex summary, cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM12). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task map, or non-empirical) apply. Thus it meets all listed inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (pictures of spicy vs non-spicy foods). It reports a within-subject contrast (spicy food > non-spicy food), and group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses with FWE correction and cluster thresholds; results are reported with coordinates and regions. Methods report participant age/sex, cue type (food), 3T scanner field strength, and processing software (SPM12). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual drug (cocaine) and natural reward (sexual) cues in 22 male cocaine patients. The paper reports within-participant, group-level voxelwise contrasts (cocaine vs. neutral; sex vs. neutral) analyzed with SPM2 and whole-brain voxelwise maps (thresholding and peak MNI coordinates provided and figures described). Participant age/sex, cue types, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM2) are reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for fMRI whole-brain cue-reactivity studies and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not non-empirical).

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) visual cue-reactivity in healthy adults. It reports within-subject contrasts (high-hedonic foods > utilitarian foods; utilitarian > neutral objects), performs group-level whole-brain random-effects voxelwise analyses using SPM2 with FDR correction, and provides participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria and violates none of the exclusions.

This study is not an fMRI cue-reactivity study. It is a tDCS neuromodulation trial measuring subjective appetite, cognitive and motor outcomes in people with obesity; no task-evoked fMRI data, whole-brain activation maps, or within-participant fMRI contrasts (e.g., food cues > control) are reported. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria requiring fMRI cue-reactivity (I3) and whole-brain task activation results (I5/I4). It is excluded on the basis that it does not provide the required fMRI activation data for inclusion in the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (chocolate pictures) contrasted with neutral control pictures and measuring cue-reactivity. The paper reports within-subject contrasts (chocolate > neutral) and performs group-level, whole-brain voxelwise regression analyses (cluster-level correction via Monte Carlo simulation) linking BOLD responses to subsequent chocolate intake. Methods report participant demographics (female undergraduates), cue type (food/chocolate), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager). Results are presented as whole-brain ROIs/clusters and described in text and figures/tables. Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria and none of the exclusion criteria.

Although this is an fMRI study using visual smoking cues (I3) and reports whole-brain task-evoked maps (interoceptive attention contrast) and participant information (I5, I7), it does not report any within-subject fMRI contrast of drug cues > control stimuli (no group-level, whole-brain cue-reactivity map for smoking images). The smoking pleasantness task is reported behaviorally, but no task-evoked smoking cue > control activation map is provided for inclusion in a cue-reactivity meta-analysis. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I4 and is excluded. Closest matching exclusion criterion: E4 (study emphasizes seed/resting connectivity and other task contrasts but lacks the required whole-brain cue-reactivity activation map).

This is an fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses and reports necessary methodological details. However, the manuscript does not report any within-participant contrast of reward cues > control stimuli (e.g., appetizing food > unappetizing food or food > non-food) at the whole-brain level. The reported analyses focus on instructional reappraisal contrasts (e.g., costs of eating > imagine eating) rather than a cue-reactivity contrast of palatable food versus control. Because it lacks a reported within-subject cue>control task activation contrast, it fails to meet inclusion criterion I4 and therefore cannot be included in the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (food videos). The paradigm includes within-participant contrasts of food-video epochs versus control-video epochs. Analyses were conducted as group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps using SPM2 with voxel-level FWE correction and cluster reporting; results/coordinates are reported (Table 2/ROIs). Relevant details are provided: participant age and sex (women, 18–45), cue type (food), scanner field strength (4-T), and processing software (SPM2). The study is empirical and not ROI-only or connectivity-only. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This study reports a task-based fMRI investigation of methamphetamine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (pictures) and reports a within-participant contrast of Methamphetamine Cue > Control Cue. Results are presented as whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses (cluster-corrected Z>2.3, p<.05) with coordinates/tables and figures. The manuscript reports participant age/sex, cue type (methamphetamine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). All required inclusion criteria are satisfied and no exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are met.

This is an empirical fMRI study that measured cue-reactivity to e-cigarette cues using visual stimuli (images of e-cigarettes) and olfactory stimuli. Within-participant contrasts were performed comparing sweet-paired, nicotine-paired, and sweet+nicotine-paired cues versus control e-cigarettes, and group-level statistical maps were reported. The paper includes whole-brain analyses (and reports whole-brain-corrected correlations for liking) in addition to ROI/SVC results. Relevant methodological details are provided: participant age and sex, cue types (nicotine and sweet/food-related), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM12). Therefore all required inclusion criteria are satisfied and no exclusion criteria are met.

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual presentation (participants held a cigarette and viewed a live video feed), includes a within-participant cigarette>control contrast (paired t-test/manipulation check), and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses with cluster-correction. Methods specify 3T scanner and processing software (AFNI, FSL, etc.), and participant demographic/sex information are described. Whole-brain results are described in the text/tables. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7.

This study does not meet the review’s core topic inclusion criteria. It is an fMRI study of monetary reward/punishment processing (using colored-circle cues predicting gains/losses), not a cue-reactivity study presenting drug-related or natural reward–related visual cues (nicotine, food, sexual, etc.). Therefore it fails I3 and I4 (no drug/natural reward visual cue-reactivity nor within-participant contrasts of drug/natural cues>control). Although whole-brain voxelwise analyses and participant details (age/sex, scanner field strength, AFNI processing) are reported (I5/I7), the primary content does not address visual drug or natural reward cue-reactivity required for the meta-analysis objective, so it must be excluded.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (appetizing vs. plain) as natural reward stimuli in a within-subject food-choice task. The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing appetizing and plain food cues and whole-brain, group-level voxelwise statistical results (FWE/FDR-corrected) with anatomical coordinates and supplementary tables. Key methodological details required by the meta-analysis (participant age/sex, cue type: food, scanner field strength 3.0T, and processing software SPM8) are provided. Results include both within-group and between-group whole-brain activations and correlations with behavioral craving measures, and whole-brain maps/coordinates are available in supplementary materials. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward stimuli (romantic/sexual images) with within-participant contrasts of romantic>neutral. Analyses used whole-brain, group-level random-effects voxelwise testing (SPM8) with cluster correction and report task-evoked activations across the brain. Participant age/sex, cue type (sexual/romantic), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software are reported. Thus it meets the meta-analysis requirements for visual natural reward cue-reactivity and provides whole-brain results suitable for coordinate-based inclusion.

This is an fMRI study of food (natural reward) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with within-participant contrasts (high-calorie vs objects/low-calorie) and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses. However, the manuscript does not report the MRI scanner field strength in the provided full text, and inclusion criterion I7 requires reporting participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software. Because scanner field strength is not reported, not all required inclusion criteria are satisfied, so the study must be excluded.

This is an fMRI study using visual smoking cues (nicotine) as distractors during a working memory task. The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing smoking-cue trials to control (no-distractor) trials and neutral cues, using voxelwise whole-brain GLM and group-level repeated-measures ANOVA with cluster-correction. Results include tables and figures reporting coordinates and statistical maps (e.g., Tables 3–4, Figures 1–3) and report participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BrainVoyager). Thus it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for group-level whole-brain task activation contrasts of drug cue>control using visual stimuli.

This is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult never-smokers using visual food cues (natural reward). It includes within-subject contrasts of food>control pictures, reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (exploratory whole-brain thresholds and tables/coordinates reported), and provides participant age and sex, cue type (food; nicotine manipulation reported), and MRI field strength (3T). Processing details are indicated (use of PickAtlas and first-/second-level GLMs; full processing details appear referenced in Supplementary Information). Therefore it satisfies the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for visual cue-reactivity fMRI studies and reports whole-brain results and necessary participant/scanner information. Confidence slightly reduced because explicit naming of the primary fMRI processing software is not present in the main text but is likely provided in the supplement.

This study reports an fMRI experiment using visual methamphetamine-cue pictures in methamphetamine-dependent participants and healthy controls. It includes within-participant contrasts (methamphetamine task vs rest baseline) and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses conducted in SPM5 with an explicit voxelwise threshold and cluster extent. Participant age and sex, cue type (methamphetamine), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not resting-state/connectivity-only, includes unmasked whole-brain task effects).

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward). It reports within-subject contrasts of hedonic food images > non-food objects (and hedonic > neutral food), conducted at the group level with voxelwise whole-brain random-effects analyses using SPM5 and cluster-level correction. Results include whole-brain activations with coordinates and t-values, plus an ROI supplement; participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software are reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes unmasked whole-brain task effects).

This is a task-based functional MRI study of smoking (nicotine) cue reactivity using visual picture stimuli. The study reports within-subject contrasts of smoking vs neutral cues and presents a group-level, whole-brain voxelwise ANOVA with FWE-corrected results and clusters described in the text/tables. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is nicotine, scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (AFNI, FSL for VBM, TBSS for DTI) are specified. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis and does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not resting/seed-only, includes whole-brain task activation maps).

This is an empirical fMRI study of visual natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using a within-subject high-caloric > low-caloric contrast. Analyses were whole-brain, voxelwise group-level SPM12 results with cluster-level FWE correction and reported cluster/coordinate information. Participant age and sex (male-only), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12) are all provided. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical). Therefore the study meets all listed inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward). It includes within-subject contrasts of high-hedonic food images versus non-food objects (H>O), reports group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses (voxelwise maps, FDR-corrected), and also provides figures/tables and methodological details. Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply.

This paper is a trial protocol describing planned fMRI cue-reactivity methods in smokers but reports no empirical fMRI results, statistical maps, coordinates, or within-participant contrast outcomes. The meta-analysis requires original task-evoked whole-brain activation data or reported coordinates from an empirical study. As a non-empirical protocol without original fMRI data in healthy adults, it meets exclusion criterion E6 (non-empirical paper without original fMRI data). Although the protocol specifies visual smoking cues and planned within-subject contrasts (negative smoke > positive smoke), no results are provided for inclusion.

This study is an fMRI task study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-participant contrasts (fattening>objects, nonfattening>objects, fattening>nonfattening). The paper reports group-level voxelwise (whole-brain) exploratory analyses and corrected Z statistic images and provides anatomical coordinates/locations; whole-brain results are described in the text and supplemental materials. Participant age range and sex distribution are reported, cue type is food, and processing/analysis methods (e.g., Local Analysis of Mixed Effects, Harvard-Oxford atlas) are described; scanner acquisition and processing details are referenced in the Supplementary Methods (acceptable per criteria I6). Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria for fMRI visual natural reward cue-reactivity and reports whole-brain task-evoked results suitable for meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. Participants passively viewed food images and the authors report a within-participant contrast of food cues > control (nature) images. Subject-level contrasts were submitted to a second-level, group T-map (whole-brain voxelwise analysis). Methods report participant age and sex (75 female college students, ages 18–23), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T Philips), and processing software (SPM8). Although an IFG ROI was examined, whole-brain group results were generated and used in analyses, so the study is not ROI-only. Therefore all inclusion criteria are met.

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity that includes visual stimuli (high-ED vs low-ED food images). It reports within-participant contrasts of high-ED > low-ED, and performs group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using SPM8 with cluster correction and reporting of activation loci. Key methodological details required by the meta-analysis are provided: participant age and sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software. Therefore all inclusion criteria for the review are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

This fMRI study used visual smoking-related stimuli (tailored and untailored smoking-cessation messages) contrasted against neutral control messages within subjects (smoking-cessation messages > neutral), and the amygdala and other brain regions were analyzed. The paper reports voxel-wise, whole-brain exploratory results (coordinates, cluster extents, significance thresholds) in addition to anatomically defined ROI analyses. Participant age and sex are reported, cue type is nicotine-related, scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (SPM) are specified. Therefore it satisfies the meta-analysis requirements for visual drug-related cue-reactivity, a within-participant drug-cues>control contrast, whole-brain voxelwise results (reported in text/tables/figures), and reporting of required methodological/participant details.

This is an fMRI study of visual natural reward (food) cues with within-subject contrasts and relevant participant/cue/scanner details, but the neuroimaging results are reported using an a priori ROI approach with small-volume correction. The Methods state analyses were run within an ROI mask (WFU Pickatlas) and SVC in SPM8; no unmasked, whole-brain (voxelwise) group-level task activation map or whole-brain correction is described. Therefore the study reports only ROI-based task results and does not provide the required whole-brain task-evoked statistical map, violating inclusion criterion I5 and meeting exclusion criterion E3.

This is an fMRI study of visual natural reward cues (food) in adults and reports participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software. However, the paper’s analyses are exclusively seed-based task-dependent connectivity (PPI) comparing oxytocin vs placebo; it does not present group-level whole-brain, task-evoked activation maps or within-participant contrasts of high-calorie cue > control stimuli as whole-brain voxelwise results needed for inclusion. Although a prior publication from the same dataset reported BOLD activation, those whole-brain task maps are not provided in this manuscript. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for studies reporting only seed-based/effective connectivity without accompanying whole-brain task activation maps (E4).

Full text was not accessible (site content failed to load), so I cannot verify whether this study meets critical inclusion criteria. The title suggests an fMRI cue-reactivity study in heroin users, but the provided content does not confirm: I3 (use of visual stimuli), I4 (within-subject contrast drug cues > control), I5/I6 (reporting of whole-brain, group-level voxelwise results or coordinates), or I7 (reporting of participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software). Because ALL inclusion criteria must be verifiable and at least one could not be confirmed from the provided information, I must exclude the study. If the full text or supplemental materials become available, re-screening could be done.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food stimuli (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts comparing high-calorie (food) vs low-calorie/control images (e.g., snack go vs vegetable go). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using FSL (Z>2.3, cluster p<0.05 corrected) and results are reported with coordinates, figures, and ROI follow-ups. Relevant metadata are provided: participant age and sex, cue type (food), 3T scanner field strength, and processing software. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state only, includes whole-brain task activation maps). Thus all required inclusion criteria (I3–I7) are met.

This is an fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (subliminal sexual images) with healthy adult participants (n=37, males). It reports within-subject contrasts of sexual cues > neutral (Sex vs Neu) and Sex vs Fix, and presents group-level whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps (one-sample t-tests and cluster results for Sex vs Neu) with reported clusters/local maxima. Scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (FSL/FEAT) are reported, as are participant age/sex and cue type. Although some group differences were analyzed with an ROI mask, the study includes qualifying whole-brain results and coordinate/cluster reporting. Therefore it meets all specified inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) and includes within-subject contrasts of high-energy food vs. neutral and low-energy vs. neutral. Group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM12 with a whole-brain FDR threshold and reported cluster results. Relevant methodological details are provided (cue type: food; MRI scanner: 3 T Siemens TRIO; processing software: SPM12), and participant sex is specified (female). Therefore the study meets all required inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity meta-analysis of visual natural rewards.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward cue-reactivity (food) using visual stimuli and reports within-subject contrasts of food>objects and whole-brain group analyses. However, the full text as provided does not report the MRI scanner field strength (required information per I7). Because all inclusion criteria must be met, and I7 (reporting scanner field strength alongside age/sex, cue type, and processing software) is not satisfied in the provided text, the study must be excluded. No exclusion criteria (E3–E6) are triggered.

This is an empirical fMRI study examining cue-reactivity to visual natural rewards (sexual images) and monetary rewards. The manuscript reports within-subject contrasts comparing Sexual (CS+Sex) versus Neutral (CS−) stimuli and outcomes, with group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (whole-brain FWE cluster-corrected results and MNI coordinates are reported). Scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (SPM8) are specified; participant sex and age characterization and cue type (sexual) are described. Therefore the study meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of fMRI cue-reactivity to natural rewards using visual stimuli.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual tobacco (e-cigarette) advertising cues to assess cue-reactivity in young adults (nicotine-related stimuli). It reports within-subject contrasts comparing flavored/tobacco e-cigarette images versus control images, and includes group-level whole-brain analyses (cluster-corrected results with coordinates referenced in supplement). Participant age and sex information, cue type (e-cigarette/nicotine), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12) are all reported. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for fMRI visual cue-reactivity studies and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study that uses visual drug-related cues (images of cocaine/crack/IV/non‑IV) versus neutral images (water) within a within‑subject design. The GLM explicitly models drug-incentive anticipation events and contrasts (premature vs. correct within drug trials) and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps (cluster-forming z>2.3, p<.05) including drug‑condition activations and SDI>HC group comparisons. The paper provides coordinates, figures, and ROI/whole-brain results, and reports participant demographics (age/sex), cue types, scanner field strength (Siemens TIM Trio 3T), and processing software (FSL, SPM12). Thus it meets the review’s requirements for an fMRI visual cue‑reactivity study with within‑participant contrasts, whole‑brain results, and necessary methodological details.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual sexual images (a natural reward) as cues and neutral control images. The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing sexual > neutral images for both the line-orientation (Sex LO > Neutral LO) and picture-categorization (Sex PC > Neutral PC) tasks. Group-level second-level analyses were performed (one-sample and two-sample t-tests) and the manuscript states that whole-brain analyses are reported in the online supplementary material, fulfilling the requirement for a group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked map. Participant age and sex are reported (n=97, 47 men, 50 women; mean age 25.3±4.5), cue type (sexual), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (SPM12) are all documented. Although ROI analyses are presented, whole-brain results are available; no exclusion criteria are met. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

The study used gustatory (taste) high-calorie cues delivered as liquids (personalized beverages) rather than visual cue stimuli. The meta-analysis specifically requires fMRI cue-reactivity studies using visual stimuli (I3). Although the paper includes a within-participant contrast of high-calorie cues > water (I4), reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise activation maps (I5/I6), and provides methodological details (scanner 3T, FSL processing) (I6), the primary cue modality does not meet the required visual-stimulus criterion. Therefore it must be excluded.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual sexual cues (pornographic vs. neutral images) and reports within-subject contrasts (explicit>neutral) and group-level, whole-brain random effects analyses (FWE-corrected clusters). Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (sexual), MRI scanner (3T Siemens Skyra) and processing software (SPM12) are reported. Results include whole-brain activation maps and coordinate tables and also report PPI connectivity but not exclusively. Thus all inclusion criteria are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

This study used fMRI with visual food (natural reward) cues in children and reports contrasts of portion-size conditions, but the neuroimaging results are presented using an ROI approach rather than group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation maps. The manuscript states the authors previously published a whole-brain analysis on a subset, but the present full-cohort analyses relied on ROI contrasts and do not provide unmasked, whole-brain group-level statistical maps (voxelwise results) or coordinates for a whole-brain task effect. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence and meets the exclusion criterion for studies reporting only ROI analyses without whole-brain results.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (desserts and vegetables) assessing natural reward-related cue-reactivity. Subject-level contrasts included food cue conditions versus rest (nogo_dessert vs rest; nogo_vegetable vs rest), and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps were reported (pre- vs post-surgery difference maps; whole-brain thresholding reported). Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM12) are reported. Although the primary group-level tests examine pre-post differences, the underlying within-participant cue>control contrasts and whole-brain group maps satisfy the meta-analysis requirements.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) using visual stimuli and includes within-participant contrasts of hedonic > neutral foods, and reports participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). However, the analyses are ROI-based (a priori ROIs with mean values extracted) and the authors explicitly note they did not perform a whole-brain analysis due to sample size. No group-level, voxelwise whole-brain statistical map is reported. Thus the study violates the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked results and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting).
