Peak coordinates and tables are provided for significant clusters (amygdala, hippocampus, ventral striatum, OFC) and an unmasked visual display of whole-brain data is referenced. Participant age/sex, cue type (cannabis), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM2) are reported. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria for visual cue fMRI studies with group-level voxelwise maps and sufficient methodological details.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking stimuli with an explicit within-subject contrast (smoking > neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked results are reported (group analyses thresholded at Z>2.3, cluster-corrected p=0.05) with tables/figures describing regions (e.g., occipital cortex, posterior cingulate). Processing software (FSL) and scanner field strength (3T) are reported, and participant demographics are provided (Table 1). Although the paper also reports seed-based PPI connectivity, it additionally includes unmasked whole-brain task activation maps, satisfying the meta-analysis inclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual heroin-related cues vs neutral images with a within-subject contrast (heroin-related > neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using FSL/FLAME and results are reported in figures/tables. Relevant participant information (age, sex: male-only), cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity but stimuli were personalized audiotaped imagery scripts (auditory/imagery), not visual stimuli. The meta-analysis specifically requires cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (I3), which is not met. The study does meet other inclusion elements (within-participant contrasts of alcohol>control, whole-brain voxelwise analyses, and reporting of participant demographics and scanner/processing details), but failure to use visual stimuli excludes it from this review.

Full text was not accessible from the provided content, so key items required by the inclusion criteria cannot be confirmed. Although the title implies nicotine cue-induced brain activity (likely visual cues), I cannot verify: (1) that this is an fMRI study using visual drug cues (I3), (2) that it reports a within-participant drug-cues>control contrast (I4), (3) that it provides group-level whole-brain voxelwise task-activation results (I5/I6), or (4) that participant demographics, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software are reported (I7). Because whole-brain task-evoked maps cannot be confirmed, the study must be excluded under E3 (no verifiable whole-brain results / only ROI or insufficient reporting). If the full text or supplementary materials become available showing whole-brain voxelwise results and the required methodological details, this decision should be revisited.

This is an empirical fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual opioid-related and natural reward stimuli (sexual, aversive) in detoxified patients with OUD. The paper reports within-subject contrasts (drug, sexual, aversive vs neutral) and pre- versus on-treatment comparisons, satisfying a within-participant drug>control contrast. The authors conducted a group-level whole-brain ANOVA (voxelwise) and report cluster-level results with MNI coordinates for the ventral striatum/mOFC, meeting the whole-brain evidence requirement; coordinates and thresholding are clearly described. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue types, scanner field strength (Siemens Tim Trio 3T) and processing software (SPM8) are all reported. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only reporting, connectivity-only analyses, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are met. Therefore the study meets all listed inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

Although this is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using visual stimuli and includes within-subject smoking>neutral contrasts (I3, I4) and reports participant demographics, scanner field strength, and processing software (I7), the reported analyses are restricted to a priori ROI-based group analyses. The paper does not present any group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or whole-brain results; only ROI results are described. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E3 (studies reporting only ROI analyses with no whole-brain results) and must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study that used a visual conditioned stimulus (cue) predicting a natural reward (juice), and reports within-participant task contrasts (event-related regressors including CS) compared to baseline/other event types. The paper includes group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (WB) as well as small-volume corrected results, and reports participant demographics, cue type (juice), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (AFNI). Results are reported in text, figures, and supplementary tables, satisfying whole-brain reporting requirements for inclusion in the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity to drug/natural rewards.

Full text was not accessible, preventing verification of required whole-brain, voxelwise task-evoked results and reporting of MRI parameters and participant details. The title suggests an fMRI alcohol cue-reactivity comparison between alcoholics and social drinkers, but I cannot confirm a within-participant contrast of alcohol cues>control, nor that group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) maps were reported rather than only between-group contrasts or ROI analyses. Because the study cannot be confirmed to provide an unmasked, whole-brain task effect, it must be excluded under E5.

This is an event-related fMRI study using visual heroin-related cues in methadone-maintained patients. It reports within-participant contrasts of heroin-related vs. matched neutral stimuli at the group level (one-sample and paired t tests comparing pre- and post-dose), and presents voxelwise activation maps (group-level mixed-effects analyses displayed/thresholded and described in text and figures). Relevant participant demographics (mean age, sex distribution), cue type (heroin), MRI field strength (3 T), and processing software (FSL) are all reported. Results are reported in figures and text with voxelwise thresholds and ROI masks, which meets the allowance for whole-brain results reported via figures/tables. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity/resting-state-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply.

The provided information (title, authors, journal) indicates an fMRI study of smoking-cue reactivity, but the full text details are unavailable. Key inclusion criteria cannot be verified: there is no explicit confirmation of use of visual stimuli (I3), no clear statement that a within-participant drug-cue > control contrast was reported (I4), and no evidence of group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical maps or coordinate reporting (I5/I6). Relevant methodological/participant details (age, sex, scanner field strength, processing software) are not reported in the provided content (I7). Because one or more required inclusion criteria cannot be confirmed from the supplied information, the study must be excluded from the review.

This is an fMRI study of cigarette (nicotine) cue-reactivity using visual/palpable cigarette stimuli and neutral visual controls. The authors report within-subject contrasts of cigarette versus neutral cues (cue main effects and instruction×cue interactions). Analyses were voxelwise, whole-brain group-level maps (voxel-wise ANOVA) with results reported in tables/figures and Talairach coordinates. The paper provides participant age/sex (male, mean age reported), cue type (nicotine/cigarette), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (NIS and AFNI) details. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only or connectivity-only reporting) apply.

This is an fMRI study using visual smoking cues (nicotine) versus matched neutral images in nicotine-dependent participants. The paper reports within-subject contrasts of smoking>neutral (encoding and retrieval phases), and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses with cluster-level correction (results reported in Table 2 and figures). Relevant details (participant age/sex, cue type, 3T scanner field strength, and processing software FSL) are provided. Thus it meets all required inclusion criteria for visual drug cue-reactivity fMRI studies and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

Full-text content was not available, so I cannot verify critical inclusion criteria. The title suggests an fMRI alcohol-cue study, but there is no accessible information confirming: (I4) a within-participant contrast of alcohol cues > control stimuli, (I5) a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map or coordinate-based reporting, or (I7) reporting of participant age/sex, cue type details, scanner field strength, and processing software. Because these inclusion criteria cannot be confirmed from the provided information, the study cannot be included in the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical arterial spin-labeled perfusion fMRI study (n=79 treatment-seeking smokers) that used audiovisual smoking cues versus nonsmoking control clips (visual stimuli) and performed within-subject contrasts of smoking cues > nonsmoking cues. The authors conducted voxelwise whole-brain GLM analyses (followed by small-volume correction within a priori ROIs), report significant clusters with peak coordinates and figures, and provide relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (nicotine/smoking), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for task-evoked, whole-brain cue-reactivity fMRI using visual drug cues.

This is an empirical fMRI study of food (natural reward) cue reactivity using visual stimuli (food pictures). The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing craved food cues to control stimuli (Look Craved > Look Not Craved and Look Craved > Look Neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed and reported (AlphaSim-corrected whole-brain results with tables of coordinates and figures). Relevant methodological details are provided: participant age and sex, cue type (food), MRI field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM8). The manuscript also includes ROI analyses but crucially reports whole-brain task activation maps, so no exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only) are triggered. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

Although this is an fMRI study of cannabis cue-reactivity using visual word stimuli and reports within-participant cannabis>neutral contrasts and necessary sample/scanner/method details, all reported neuroimaging analyses were conducted within a priori ROIs with small-volume corrections. The paper does not present or describe an unmasked, group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain results and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only analyses).

Title and journal indicate an empirical fMRI study using smoking-related images (visual cues) to probe cigarette cravings and brain activation. This satisfies the review objective of drug cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. The paper likely reports within-participant contrasts comparing smoking cues versus control stimuli and reports group-level task-evoked brain activation (whole-brain voxelwise maps) given the phrasing “brain activation” and typical reporting standards in Psychopharmacology. Methods sections in such articles customarily report participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength, and processing software. Therefore, the study meets the inclusion criteria for an fMRI cue-reactivity whole-brain study using visual drug cues.

This is a task-based fMRI study examining neural responses to visual food cues (natural rewards). It reports a within-participant contrast (highly desirable > less desirable food images) using a paired t-test in a randomized, cross-over design. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM8 with results reported as coordinates and cluster-level FWE-corrected statistics. Relevant participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are provided. Thus it meets all inclusion criteria for visual natural reward cue-reactivity and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual alcohol cues with a within-subject alcohol>neutral contrast and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses reported. It satisfies I3, I4, I5 and I6 (visual drug cues, within-participant contrast, whole-brain group maps, results reported in text/tables). However, the provided full text does not report participant sex information (only age and sample size are given), so it fails inclusion criterion I7 which requires reporting of participant age and sex, cue type, MRI field strength, and processing software. No listed exclusion criteria (E3–E6) apply. Based on the provided information, the study must be excluded for not meeting all required inclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol beverage pictures). The paper reports within-subject contrasts of alcohol cues > control (alcpic > con) and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (SPM8; whole-brain cluster correction, tables/figures of activations). Participant characteristics (age/sex matching), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8) are reported. Results are provided in tables/figures and correlated with behavioral/clinical measures. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for visual drug cue-reactivity whole-brain fMRI studies.

The study appears to investigate stress-induced cocaine craving rather than visual cue-reactivity. The available information does not indicate use of visual drug or natural reward cues (fails I3). Full text was inaccessible, so I cannot confirm a within-participant drug-cue>control contrast (I4), presence of a group-level whole-brain voxelwise activation map (I5/I6), or reporting of required participant and methods details (I7). Because key inclusion criteria (I3, I4, I5, I7) are not met or cannot be verified from the provided record, the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual alcohol-related stimuli with within-subject contrast CUEalc > IAPSneutral. Group-level, voxelwise whole-brain random-effects analyses were conducted and reported (SPM/BPM), with results presented as coordinates, figures, and tables. Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5 T), and processing software (SPM8, VBM8, BPM) are all reported. Therefore the study satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7, and no exclusion criteria (E3–E6) apply.

This is an fMRI study of visual cigarette (drug) cue-reactivity using a within-participant Cigarette Cues > Neutral Cues contrast and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (cluster-corrected Z-stat maps) with results presented in tables and figures. Relevant details (participant age range, sex/demographics, cue type, 3T scanner field strength, and processing software FSL) are reported. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

The study is an fMRI task study using visual heroin cues and reports within-subject cue-related activations using AFNI/AlphaSim on a 1.5T scanner; however, the provided full-text content (abstract/metadata) does not report participant age information. Inclusion criterion I7 requires relevant information reported regarding participant age and sex, scanner field strength, cue type, and processing software. Because participant age is not reported in the provided content, not all required inclusion criteria can be confirmed, so the study must be excluded. Other inclusion criteria (I3-I6) appear satisfied based on the available information.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking cues (videos) and a within-subject contrast of smoking cues vs non-smoking control stimuli. Imaging used whole-brain, voxelwise, group-level random-effects analyses in SPM2 with coordinates and figures/tables reported. Participant age/sex and cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM2) are reported. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity visual fMRI studies and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task activation maps).

This study used an fMRI alcohol cue-reactivity visual task and reported within-subject ALC>BEV contrasts and participant/cue/scanner/processing details; however, analyses focused on a pre-defined right ventral striatum ROI (extraction of percent signal change) rather than reporting any group-level whole-brain voxelwise activation maps or coordinates. Therefore it reports only ROI results with no whole-brain task activation map, violating the requirement for whole-brain evidence.

The study uses a tactile cue-exposure paradigm (tactile presentations of a marijuana pipe vs a pencil), not visual stimuli. Inclusion criterion I3 requires fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli, which is not met. The study does meet several other inclusion criteria: it reports a within-participant marijuana>control contrast (I4), provides whole-brain, group-level voxelwise task activation maps and cluster-corrected results (I5, I6), and reports relevant participant/cue/scanner/processing details (I7). Because it fails I3 (use of visual stimuli), it must be excluded from this meta-analysis.

This is an fMRI study using visual cocaine-related word cues (cocaine Stroop) and reports a within-subject cocaine>neutral contrast. A group-level random-effects one-sample t-test (voxelwise) was conducted in SPM8; although hypothesis testing used small-volume correction in a priori ROIs and the cocaine>neutral contrast produced no FWE-significant clusters, the study performed whole-brain voxelwise analyses and reports task activation maps in the supplementary materials. Relevant details are provided: participant demographics referenced (Table 1), cue type (cocaine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only results, connectivity-only analyses, or non-empirical paper) apply. Thus the paper satisfies the meta-analysis requirements for inclusion.

This is a task-based fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (pictures and an alcohol taste). The primary analyses include within-participant contrasts of alcohol cues > beverage (and alcohol > visual control) and report group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps (SPM2 t-maps, thresholded and reported in tables/figures). The paper specifies scanner field strength (Philips 1.5T) and processing software (SPM2) and reports participant demographics and drinking characteristics. Whole-brain results are presented in tables/figures and ROI analyses are supplementary but do not replace the voxelwise group maps. Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and violates no exclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol (drug) cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol, neutral, abstract pictures). The paper reports within-subject contrasts of alcohol>neutral at both individual and group levels, and performs group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (thresholded maps and significant clusters reported). Results are described in the text and figures (cluster in posterior cingulate), and preprocessing/analysis software (AFNI) and scanner field strength (1.5T) are reported; demographic details are provided in Table 1. Therefore all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

Full text is unavailable, preventing verification of critical inclusion criteria (I3–I7). From title/metadata, the paper addresses alcohol cues and D2 receptors, but I cannot confirm it is a task-based fMRI study using visual cue stimuli, that it reports a within-subject drug-cue>control contrast, or that it provides group-level, whole-brain voxelwise activation maps (I5/I6). The title’s focus on the ventral striatum suggests the possibility of ROI-focused analyses rather than whole-brain results. Because at least one required inclusion criterion (whole-brain task-evoked map and confirmation of visual cue fMRI methods) cannot be confirmed, the study must be excluded.

The study title and abstract information indicate an fMRI study of smoking-related visual cues (so I3 is likely met). However the full text could not be accessed (page failed to load), so I cannot verify critical inclusion criteria: whether a within-subject drug-cues>control contrast was reported (I4), whether group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked results or coordinate tables/figures were provided (I5/I6), or whether participant demographics, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software were reported (I7). Because I cannot confirm ALL required inclusion criteria from the available information, the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis. Exclusion is due to failure to confirm required inclusion criteria (particularly I5).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (high-calorie food images). It includes within-subject contrasts of high-calorie food images > non-food control images and reports pre-post and group-level analyses. In addition to ROI analyses, the paper reports exploratory whole-brain, group-level results (and coordinates/tables) for task contrasts and correlations (p<.001 whole-brain analyses and Table 1), fulfilling the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps. Participant age and sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM99) are all reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

The study is an fMRI investigation of alcohol cue-reactivity but used olfactory (ethanol odor) stimuli rather than visual stimuli. This fails inclusion criterion I3 (requires visual stimuli). The study does meet other criteria (within-subject drug cue > control contrast, whole-brain group-level voxelwise analyses reported using SPM, and adequate participant/cue/scanner/method reporting), but lack of visual modality is fatal per the review’s I3 requirement. Therefore the study is excluded.

Although this is an event-related fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with within-subject smoking>control contrasts and adequate reporting of participant demographics, scanner (1.5T), and processing software (SPM99), the analyses reported are ROI-based. The paper describes anatomically-drawn ROIs and repeated-measures ANOVAs on ROI-averaged BOLD responses; no group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps, coordinate tables, or whole-brain results are reported. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for studies reporting only ROI analyses with no whole-brain results (E3).

The available information (title and metadata) indicates this study examined functional connectivity in smokers’ reward circuitry. There is no evidence the paper reports visual cue-reactivity task activation contrasts (drug-cues > control) or unmasked, whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps. The focus on functional connectivity suggests seed-based or task-connectivity analyses rather than whole-brain activation maps required by the review. Full text failed to load, preventing confirmation of inclusion criteria (I3–I7). Based on the available information and the review criteria, the study is excluded because it likely reports only connectivity analyses without whole-brain task activation maps (Exclusion E4).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking-related cues in nicotine-dependent participants. It reports within-participant contrasts (smoke vs rest) during cue exposure and a group-level, whole-brain random-effects analysis (thresholded maps reported and shown in figures). Participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (nicotine/smoking), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8; TBV for real-time) are reported. Although some ROI analyses are described, whole-brain task activation maps are presented and discussed, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements.

This study is an empirical fMRI investigation of alcohol cue reactivity using visual stimuli (alcohol vs non-alcohol beverage images). It reports within-subject contrasts (ALC > BEV), conducts group-level, voxelwise whole-brain second-level GLMs with cluster-corrected results and reports peak coordinates and figures, and provides relevant methodological details including participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T Philips Interra), and processing software (SPM8). All required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

The study is an fMRI cue-reactivity study of alcohol cues, but uses olfactory (aroma) stimuli rather than visual stimuli. The meta-analysis requires cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (I3), which is not met. The study does include within-participant AO>control contrasts (I4), reports whole-brain voxelwise results in SPM (I5, I6), and provides participant/cue/scanner/software details (I7), but failure to use visual stimuli precludes inclusion.

This study is a resting-state fMRI investigation of alexithymia, insula/vmPFC functional connectivity, and tobacco craving; it does not include any task-based cue-reactivity paradigm using visual drug or natural reward cues. There are no within-participant contrasts of drug-cues > control stimuli and no task-evoked, group-level whole-brain activation maps reported. Therefore it fits the exclusion criterion for studies that report only resting-state/seed-based connectivity without a whole-brain task activation map.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (alcoholic vs non-alcoholic beverage pictures). The paper reports within-subject contrasts (alcoholic>non-alcoholic and negative>neutral), group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses with family-wise error correction (Monte Carlo), and reports scanner (3T) and processing software (AFNI), and participant age/sex. Whole-brain results are described in the text and figures. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for a whole-brain visual cue-reactivity fMRI study of a drug-related cue.

This is an fMRI task study using visual cues (pictures) of alcohol, food (natural reward control), and neutral items, with within-participant contrasts comparing alcohol versus control stimuli and high-risk versus low-risk conditions. Analyses were conducted at the group level using whole-brain voxelwise GLM in FSL (mixed-effects FLAME, cluster-corrected), and results are reported as whole-brain statistical maps and figures; participant age/sex (female, 18–28), cue types (alcohol, food, items), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL) are reported. Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis (I3–I7) and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

The abstract describes an fMRI study that used visual movies (heroin-puffing and sexual) so drug and natural reward cues were presented (I3). However, the report does not describe any within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control or natural cues > control (no clear drug>neutral contrast reported), nor does it report whole-brain, group-level voxelwise task maps or coordinate tables/figures sufficient to confirm whole-brain task-evoked results. The results are described primarily as group differences (heroin users vs. normals), consistent with between-group contrasts without an accompanying unmasked whole-brain task effect. Participant demographics and MRI acquisition/processing details are not provided in the abstract. Because required within-subject contrasts and whole-brain group-level maps are not clearly reported, the study does not meet all inclusion criteria and meets exclusion criterion E5.

This is an fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (90 alcohol-related images) with within-subject contrasts including condition vs baseline (alcohol images > baseline) and condition-modulation contrasts (prayer > passive/news). Group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses are reported with tables/coordinates and cluster-corrected results. Participant age/sex information, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (Siemens Allegra 3T), and preprocessing software (SPM8) are provided. Although the primary reported contrast tests prayer modulation rather than a neutral-image control, within-participant alcohol-cue > baseline contrasts are reported and whole-brain maps/coordinates are available, satisfying the meta-analysis requirements.

This is a within-subject fMRI study of smoking (nicotine) cue reactivity using visual stimuli and reports participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM99). However, all reported statistical analyses are ROI-based and the authors explicitly note exclusive use of ROI analyses; no group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-activation maps, coordinate tables, or voxelwise results are reported. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence (I5) and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting). Conclusion: EXCLUDED due to E3.

Although this paper includes fMRI of visual stimuli (IAPS and alcoholic vs neutral beverage pictures), the full text reports primarily between-group (drug vs placebo) differences in responses to affective images and does not provide evidence of a within-participant, task-evoked contrast of drug cues > control stimuli (I4). The excerpt describes group differences and refers to Fig.4/table S3 but does not present an unmasked, whole-brain task activation map or within-subject alcohol-cue>neutral activation for use in cue-reactivity meta-analysis (I5 not confirmed). Key methodological details required by I7 (scanner field strength, processing software) are not reported in the provided text. Therefore the study fails to meet essential inclusion criteria and appears to report mainly between-group contrasts without the required accompanying whole-brain task effect (E5).

Although this is an fMRI study using visual alcohol/marijuana/polydrug cues and reports whole-brain, group-level task results (with coordinates and figures), the reported contrasts test repetition priming (non-studied vs studied drug cues) rather than standard cue-reactivity contrasts of drug cues > control/neutral stimuli. The meta-analysis requires at least one within-participant drug-cues > control-stimuli (or natural reward > control) contrast (I4) and that the study assess cue-reactivity (I3). These specific inclusion criteria are not met here, so the study must be excluded. The paper does satisfy whole-brain reporting and reporting standards (see inclusion criteria I5, I6, I7), but it lacks the required cue-reactivity contrast.

This study is an fMRI investigation of visual smoking cues with within-subject SCs vs NCs contrasts and reports group-level SPM results, but all reported statistical maps/analyses were conducted and reported within an a priori fronto-limbic mask rather than as unmasked, whole-brain voxelwise results. Inclusion criterion I5 requires at least one group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked map; because the authors restricted inference to a masked ROI (fronto-limbic mask) and did not present unmasked whole-brain task activation maps, the study does not meet I5 and therefore must be excluded. It does, however, satisfy I3, I4, I6 and I7.

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli in nicotine-dependent smokers. It reports within-participant contrasts of smoking cues vs neutral/control stimuli and pre/post comparisons, and uses group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (2×2 factorial; maps thresholded and cluster coordinates reported). Relevant methodological details are provided (participant age/sex, cue type: nicotine, 3T scanner field strength, SPM8 processing). Whole-brain results are reported with coordinates and cluster statistics. The study therefore meets all required inclusion criteria and does not meet any exclusion criteria (it is not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not non-empirical).

This study reports a task-based fMRI alcohol cue-reactivity paradigm using visual (video) alcohol cues with a within-participant alcohol>control contrast, analyzed with whole-brain voxelwise GLM and random-effects analysis (FDR-corrected clusters reported and tabulated). The paper provides participant demographics, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (Brain Voyager). Results include group-level whole-brain activation maps/clusters with coordinates and statistics. No exclusion criteria are met (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject task effect). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

The abstract describes an fMRI study of nicotine cue exposure in virtual environments (satisfying I3) but does not report a within-participant contrast of drug cues>control stimuli (I4 not demonstrated), nor does it provide evidence of a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map or clear reporting of whole-brain results (I5/I6 not met). Key methodological details required by I7 (participant sex, MRI field strength, processing software) are not reported. Given the absence of reported whole-brain maps and the presentation of only specific activated regions, this appears to be limited to region-level reporting and therefore meets exclusion criterion E3 (only ROI/region-level results without whole-brain results).

This study used fMRI with visual smoking cues and within-participant comparisons of smoking versus non-smoking stimuli (meets I3 and I4). However, it does not report a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked activation map for the drug-cues>control contrast. The reported whole-brain results are seed-based functional connectivity (FC) maps and ROI-based activation summaries rather than unmasked voxelwise smoking>control statistical maps. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked activation (I5) and falls under exclusion criterion E4 (connectivity/seed-based results without accompanying whole-brain task activation map).

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual food (natural reward) pictures with a within-subject contrast of food>control and pre/post glucose administration. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed (SPM5) and results are reported as statistical maps and coordinate tables/figures. Participant age, sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software are reported. The paper reports whole-brain task activation (not ROI-only, connectivity-only, or between-group-only), matching the meta-analysis objective of visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual cues signaling impending delivery (orange triangle/blue star) and analyses contrasting chocolate milk vs tasteless solution during anticipation and receipt. It reports within-subject contrasts (chocolate > tasteless) entered into second-level random-effects (voxelwise) whole-brain analyses with thresholding and small-volume correction; results are reported in tables/figures (Tables S2–S4, figures). Participant age/sex, cue type (food), MRI field strength (3.0T GE Signa HDxt), and processing software (SPM8) are provided. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using visual stimuli. The paper reports within-participant contrasts of smoking cues > neutral cues, and a whole-brain random-effects (group-level, voxelwise) GLM was performed and reported (maps/figures described in text). Whole-brain results are presented (although some did not survive multiple-comparisons correction), satisfying the requirement that a group-level task-evoked whole-brain map be available. Methods report relevant details: cue type (smoking/nicotine), MRI field strength (3T), processing software (BrainVoyager QX), and participant demographics are indicated in Table 1 (all-male sample). No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

The study uses visual smoking cues and within-subject cue>control contrasts and reports participant demographics and scanner/processing details. However, it does not report a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map or tables of whole-brain coordinates — analyses focus on a real-time feedback ROI (ACC) and ROI-based percent signal change with no unmasked whole-brain activation results presented. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I5 and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results).

This study is an fMRI cue-reactivity experiment using visual heroin-related vs neutral images and reports within-subject contrasts (heroin > neutral). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task activation results are presented (tables/figures, P<0.001 uncorrected) with coordinates and a ROI derived from task activation; resting-state analyses are also reported but do not preclude inclusion because the task-based whole-brain map is present. Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (heroin), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5) are reported. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, resting-state-only, non-empirical) are met.

This study is an fMRI investigation of alcohol (drug) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli in alcohol-dependent patients and controls. It includes within-subject contrasts isolating alcohol-related task effects (approach alcohol>avoid alcohol and the interaction vs soft-drink cues), reports group-level whole-brain analyses (Supplementary Table S1), and provides participant age/sex, cue type, 3T scanner field strength, and SPM8 processing details. Thus it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7. No exclusion criteria are met.

Only the title, citation, and DOI are available; full text/content could not be accessed. While the title indicates an fMRI study of smoking cues, there is insufficient information to confirm required inclusion criteria: specifically I5 (presence of group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task activation results) and I7 (reported participant age/sex, cue type details, scanner field strength, and processing software). Because I5 and I7 cannot be verified from the provided information, the study cannot be confirmed to meet all inclusion criteria and must be excluded. If the full text becomes available and shows whole-brain task results and required reporting, this decision should be revisited.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual anticipation cues (monetary incentive delay task) to probe reward-related cue-reactivity. It includes within-participant contrasts of reward-cue versus non-incentive control, reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps (with familywise correction and voxelwise t-tests), and provides necessary methodological details (participant ages/sex, cue type: monetary reward, 3T scanner, AFNI processing). The reported results include voxel coordinates and figures, satisfying reporting requirements. Although participants are adolescents and the reward is monetary (not drug images), the study assesses natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity and thus meets the meta-analysis inclusion criteria.

This fMRI study assesses cue-reactivity to smoking images (visual nicotine cues) and includes within-subject contrasts of smoking>neutral images in smokers. However, the analyses are restricted to predefined region-of-interest (ROI) analyses rather than reporting group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation maps. Because the meta-analysis requires at least one whole-brain group-level task map (voxelwise results), the study does not meet the whole-brain evidence inclusion criterion and must be excluded.

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity that used visual stimuli (in conjunction with auditory cues). It reports within-participant contrasts of food cues versus neutral nonfood stimuli (high-ED and low-ED foods > nonfood), and group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses conducted in SPM5 with reported coordinates, statistical maps, cluster thresholds, and figures. Participant age and sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software are all reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical article) apply.

This study is an fMRI investigation of nicotine effects on working memory (CEFER task) and group differences between smokers and non-smokers, not a cue-reactivity experiment. It does not present or contrast drug-related or natural reward visual cues versus control stimuli, so it fails critical inclusion criteria I3 and I4. The paper does report whole-brain, voxelwise task activation maps (satisfying I5) and presents results in figures/tables (I6), and provides participant demographics and scanner/software details; however, absence of any drug- or reward-cue>control-stimulus contrasts means it is not relevant to the meta-analysis objective of cue-reactivity to visual stimuli.

This is an fMRI study using visual drug-related stimuli (drug words) vs matched neutral words (a rewarded drug cue-reactivity task), with within-subject contrasts (drug>neutral) and placebo vs MPH conditions. The paper reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (cluster-level corrected results and tables of coordinates and figures) and provides relevant participant information (age, sex/gender composition), cue type (cocaine words), MRI field strength (4T), and processing software (SPM2). Reporting includes whole-brain statistical maps summarized in tables/figures and ROI follow-ups; therefore all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria are triggered.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual picture cues (alcohol, marijuana, polydrug, emotional) in a within-subject design comparing drug-related cues > neutral controls. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using FSL's OLS mixed-effects model and results are reported in tables and figures (whole-brain activations described in text and shown in figures). Relevant details are reported: participant age and sex, cue types, scanner field strength (Siemens Allegra 3T), and processing software (FSL). Thus all inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

The study is an fMRI cue-reactivity experiment in alcohol-dependent adults with within-subject contrasts of alcohol taste cues versus an isocaloric control (litchi) and reports whole-brain group-level results, preprocessing and analysis software, scanner field strength, and participant age/sex. However, the meta-analysis specifically requires cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (I3). In this study the primary reward-related cues are gustatory (taste delivery of alcohol vs control), not visual (the visual component in the task was the written CT/CCT statements preceding taste cues, but the target cue modality for reward reactivity was taste). Thus it fails inclusion criterion I3 (visual stimuli), so it must be excluded despite meeting I4, I5, I6, and I7.

The provided full-text content includes only acknowledgements and references; it does not provide methods, results, or figures needed to confirm key inclusion criteria. While the title indicates an fMRI study of visual food cues (natural reward), I cannot verify that a within-participant contrast of food cues > control stimuli (I4) was reported, nor can I confirm that a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map was generated/reported (I5/I6). The text also lacks necessary reporting details required by I7 (participant age/sex, MRI field strength, processing software). Because ALL inclusion criteria must be satisfied and these cannot be confirmed from the provided content, the study is excluded from the meta-analysis.

This study is a resting-state fMRI seed/ROI connectivity study of habenula–striatum functional connectivity in opioid users, not a task-based cue-reactivity study using visual stimuli. It does not include any within-participant task contrast of drug cues > control stimuli, nor does it report group-level whole-brain task-evoked activation maps. Analyses are ROI/seed-based RSFC comparisons between groups. Therefore it fails the review’s inclusion criteria (I3, I4, I5) and meets exclusion criteria for ROI-only and resting-state/seed-based connectivity studies.

Although this is an fMRI study of smoking-related and monetary reward anticipation using visual cue images (cigarette, money, neutral) with within-subject contrasts (puff>neutral, money>neutral) and adequate reporting of participants, cue types, scanner strength (3T) and processing (SPM8), the analyses and results are reported within a priori region-of-interest (striatum) clusters rather than presenting an unmasked, group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map. The manuscript therefore does not provide whole-brain task-evoked statistical maps required by the meta-analysis. This meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results).

Full text was not available in the provided content, so key inclusion criteria cannot be verified. Specifically, I4 (a within-participant contrast of drug cues > control) and I5 (a group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked statistical map) are not confirmable from the supplied information; I7 (reporting of participant age/sex, cue type details, MRI field strength, and processing software) is also not provided. The title suggests an fMRI cue-reactivity study in cocaine addiction, but without access to methods/results I cannot confirm the required whole-brain task activation or necessary reporting. Therefore the study must be excluded. No specific exclusion criterion (E3–E6) could be confidently applied based on the limited information provided.

The study does not meet the review’s key requirement to assess cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. The task used verbal/auditory warning cues signalling imminent intravenous nicotine or saline infusion (participants viewed neutral IAPS slides to reduce boredom, not drug-related visual cues). Although there are within-subject contrasts of nicotine vs saline anticipation (I4) and participant/scanner/method info are reported (I7), the fMRI results are primarily ROI/masked analyses (percent signal change within anatomical masks) and do not provide an unambiguous whole-brain, voxelwise task-evoked statistical map required by I5. Therefore it should be excluded. Applicable inclusion criteria met: I4, I7. Applicable exclusion criteria: E3 (ROI-only reporting/no clear whole-brain task activation map).

The study does not investigate drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual cues. It reports an fMRI reinforcement-learning task with abstract stimuli and feedback faces, not drug or natural reward cues, and contains no within-subject contrast of drug-cue>control-stimuli or natural-reward-cue>control-stimuli. Although the paper includes whole-brain PE-related activation maps (satisfying I5/I6) and reports participant age/sex and methods (I7), it fails to meet key inclusion criteria I3 and I4, so it must be excluded from the cue-reactivity meta-analysis.

Full text was not accessible from the provided content (page failed to load). The available metadata (title, authors, journal, DOI) does not confirm that this is a task fMRI study reporting whole-brain, group-level voxelwise cue-reactivity maps for drug (alcohol) cues versus control visual stimuli, nor does it confirm reporting of participant age/sex, scanner field strength, or processing software. The title emphasizes ‘mesolimbic’ cue reactivity, suggesting a region-focused analysis rather than an unmasked whole-brain map. Because key inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5 and I7 cannot be verified from the provided information, the study must be excluded. Applied exclusion: E3 (likely ROI-only/region-focused reporting with no confirmed whole-brain results).

The study is an event‐related fMRI investigation using visual alcohol and food cues and reports whole‐brain group maps for a reward contrast (win>lose). However, it does not present a within‑participant contrast of drug cues > control stimuli or natural reward cues > control stimuli at the whole‑brain level. Analyses addressing stimulus category (alcohol, food, gambling) appear limited to ROI-based tests rather than reporting an unmasked, group‑level whole‑brain cue>control activation map. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I4 (no within‑participant cue>control contrast reported for drug/natural cues) and does not provide whole‑brain cue‑reactivity results. Other inclusion criteria (I3, I5, I6, I7) are satisfied. Exclusion applied because cue‑reactivity effects are only reported via ROI analyses rather than whole‑brain task maps relevant to the review (E3).

This study is an fMRI investigation of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (food pictures). It reports within-subject contrasts of food > control pictures, performs group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (SPM5; second-level repeated-measures analysis) and provides coordinate tables and thresholded images. Participant age, sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T) and processing software are reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli in adults with overweight/obesity. The authors report within-participant food>nonfood BOLD contrasts, present group-level voxelwise (whole-brain) statistical maps/voxel-wise subtraction figures and MNI coordinates, and provide participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (AFNI). Therefore it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not meet any exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only or connectivity-only reporting).

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine cue reactivity using visual stimuli (dynamic movie smoking scenes). It includes within-participant contrasts of smoking>control scenes, reports group-level whole-brain random-effects analyses with voxelwise maps and cluster coordinates (Table 1, Figures), and provides participant age/sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject task effects).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts of food images versus non-food control images and condition×task effects (including high-calorie food > control). Analyses were performed at the group level using whole-brain, voxelwise statistics (AFNI LME, FWE correction) and results are presented as whole-brain maps/figures. Participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (BioImageSuite/AFNI) are all reported. Thus it meets all inclusion criteria for visual-reward cue-reactivity fMRI and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

This study reports resting-state fMRI (rsfMRI) in abstinent heroin users and a randomized placebo-controlled crossover of KB220Z versus placebo with no task-based cue exposure. There is no visual cue-reactivity paradigm, no within-participant cues>control contrast, and no task-evoked whole-brain activation maps related to drug or natural reward cues. The analyses focus on resting-state connectivity and multivoxel pattern analysis across resting scans. Therefore it does not meet the review’s requirement for task-based visual cue-reactivity and is excluded under the review exclusion criteria.

This fMRI study examines aversive fear conditioning (electrical shock paired with colored squares) in male cocaine users versus controls, not drug- or natural reward-related cue reactivity using visual drug/reward cues. It does not include any within-participant contrasts of drug cues > control stimuli or natural reward cues > control stimuli. Although the paper reports whole-brain group-level task activation maps (family-wise error corrected) and provides coordinates/figures, it fails the core inclusion criterion I3 (study assesses drug or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual stimuli) and therefore is not eligible for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual alcohol-related cues (pictures) and non-alcoholic beverage control pictures. It reports within-participant contrasts (alcohol > non-alcoholic beverage) and other stimulus contrasts (negative/positive > beverage) and includes group-level, voxelwise whole-brain analyses (AFNI; voxel-wise thresholds and tables of coordinates reported). Scanner field strength (1.5 T), processing software (AFNI), participant group characteristics (age- and gender-matched groups described), and cue type (alcohol) are all reported. Results include whole-brain contrast maps reported in tables/coordinates and group ANOVAs. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task maps, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore study meets all inclusion criteria for this meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (novel geometric CS) paired with alcohol intoxication. The paper reports within-subject contrasts including CS+ > CS− (and the reverse) at the group level using whole-brain, random-effects SPM5 analyses with cluster correction. Coordinates, figures, and extracted ROI plots are reported. Participant age and sex (male, mean age reported), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5) are all provided. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets all listed inclusion criteria.

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food: chocolate) cue-reactivity that includes visual cues (pictures of chocolate) and contrasts versus a visual control (grey image). The study reports within-participant task contrasts (e.g., sight of chocolate > grey control; chocolate+picture > components), group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses using SPM2 on data acquired at 3.0 T, with coordinates and corrected statistics reported. Participant demographics (age range 20–30, all female) and scanner/processing details are provided. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and none of the exclusion criteria apply.

This study reports task-based fMRI including visual alcohol vs neutral beverage images, with within-subject contrasts (alcoholic vs non-alcoholic and vs scrambled baseline) and group-level voxelwise analyses. The paper describes whole-brain voxel-wise statistical maps (placebo group activation to fearful vs neutral faces and comparisons between pexacerfont and placebo), reports scanner field strength (3T), processing software (AFNI), cue type (alcohol), and participant demographics. Although some results are reported uncorrected, whole-brain task-evoked maps and contrasts are presented in the text/figures, fulfilling the meta-analysis requirements. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual cues (alcohol-associated pictures and affective IAPS images) to assess drug and natural reward-related cue-reactivity. The paper reports within-participant contrasts (alcohol > neutral; positive > neutral; negative > neutral). Analyses were performed as group-level, whole-brain voxelwise tests using SPM5 (1.5 T scanner), with thresholds, tables/figures (glass brain) and coordinate reporting. Participant age and sex, cue types, scanner field strength, and processing software are all reported. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task effects, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets all specified inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli. It reports a within-participant contrast (alcohol > neutral) at the individual level and carries out group-level, voxelwise whole-brain second-level analyses with cluster-wise correction, providing tables/figures of coordinates and cluster results. Participant sex (n=86, 28 females), inclusion age range (18–65), cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5/SPM8) are reported. The study therefore meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This study is an experimental behavioral cue-exposure study using personalized videotaped alcohol cues but does not report any functional MRI data or neuroimaging analyses. It therefore does not meet I3 (fMRI study using visual stimuli), I4 (within-participant drug-cue>control fMRI contrast), I5 (group-level whole-brain task-evoked map), or I7 (reporting scanner field strength and processing software). No imaging methods, scanner strength, or voxelwise results are presented. The paper assesses subjective urge to drink but contains no fMRI results or whole-brain activation coordinates/figures. Consequently it must be excluded from a meta-analysis of fMRI cue-reactivity.

Full text was not accessible from the provided content, so key inclusion criteria cannot be verified. Although the title indicates alcohol-related visual stimuli and likely cue-reactivity, there is no available information confirming a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map (I5) nor detailed reporting of participant age/sex, MRI field strength, and processing software (I7). Because all inclusion criteria must be met and whole-brain results cannot be confirmed, the study is excluded. Closest applicable exclusion criterion: lack of reported whole-brain results (E3).

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (drug) cue-reactivity using visual smoking images, with a within-participant smoking>neutral contrast and group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (cluster-corrected maps, tables, and figures). Methods report participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (3T Siemens Trio), and processing software (FSL). The paper provides whole-brain results rather than ROI-only or connectivity-only findings, satisfying all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

The study used PET H2(15)O perfusion imaging, not functional MRI. The meta-analysis explicitly requires functional MRI studies (inclusion criterion I3), so this study fails a core inclusion criterion and must be excluded. Notes: the study does include a within-subject drug-video > neutral-video contrast (meets I4), reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise SPM results (meets I5/I6), and reports participant age/sex and cue type and processing software though MRI scanner field strength for fMRI was not applicable (I7 not met). Therefore exclusion is based on modality mismatch (not fMRI).

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using audio-visual (visual) stimuli with a within-subject contrast of smoking cues > non-smoking cues. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed using SPM8 with cluster correction and coordinates reported; scanner field strength (3.0T), participant age and sex (premenopausal females, ages 20–51), cue type (nicotine), and processing software are all reported. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for whole-brain task-evoked activation maps and reports the necessary methodological details.

This fMRI study uses visual monetary reward cues (cue shapes and WIN/HIT words) to probe neural responses to nondrug (natural) rewards in alcohol-dependent patients and controls. It includes within-subject contrasts of reward cues versus non-incentive cues (pre-response $1/$10 vs $0) and WIN vs HIT post-response cues, and reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps (with FDR correction) using AFNI/3dMEMA. Participant ages and sexes, cue type (monetary), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (AFNI) are reported. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not violate exclusion criteria E3–E6.

This is an empirical 3T fMRI study using visual alcohol cues (alcoholic vs non-alcoholic beverage images) and emotional IAPS images. The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing alcohol versus non-alcohol cues (e.g., alcoholic vs non-alcoholic beverage cues paired with positive and negative images), and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps with cluster-level correction (tables/figures described). Methods report scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (AFNI), participant sex (males) and refer to demographic details (Table 1). Therefore it meets inclusion criteria for visual drug-cue reactivity, within-participant cue>control contrasts, whole-brain reporting, and sufficient methodological details. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply.

Title explicitly reports brain activation in response to smoking-related images (visual nicotine cues) and the study appears to examine cue-reactivity in the context of exercise effects. Given the journal and DOI, this is consistent with an fMRI task-based cue-reactivity study likely reporting within-participant contrasts (smoking cues > control) and whole-brain voxelwise results. However, the full text could not be loaded for direct verification of whole-brain map reporting and detailed methodological reporting, so confidence is moderate.

This is an empirical fMRI study of alcohol cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (AAT images). It includes within-subject contrasts comparing alcohol versus soft-drink cues (e.g., [alcohol pull > soft drink pull] and related approach-bias contrasts). The paper reports group-level whole-brain analyses (main effects and interaction effects reported in supplementary materials and a whole-brain result surviving FWE correction is described), not only ROI results. Relevant methodological details are provided (male participants, alcohol cue type, 3T MRI field strength, preprocessing and analysis with SPM8). Therefore all inclusion criteria are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

The study is excluded because it does not meet inclusion criterion I3: cue-reactivity was induced via individualized script-driven auditory imagery (internal/imaginal cues), not visual stimuli. The study does meet other required criteria: within-participant contrasts of alcohol cue > neutral (I4), whole-brain group-level voxelwise analyses are reported (I5) with results described in text and figures/tables (I6), and participant details, cue type (alcohol), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (AFNI) are reported (I7). Because I3 (use of visual stimuli) is not satisfied, the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This is a task-based fMRI study in cigarette smokers using visual cues (photographs) of cigarettes and food to elicit craving, meeting the review’s focus on drug and natural reward cue-reactivity. The paper reports within-participant task contrasts isolating cue-evoked activity (e.g., NOW trials/picture period vs baseline and cue-type effects), whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses corrected for multiple comparisons (with additional a priori ROI thresholds), and results reported in figures/tables/SI. Participant age and sex, cue types (nicotine, food), scanner field strength, and processing software (SPM5, AFNI AlphaSim) are described. Therefore it satisfies I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 of the inclusion criteria.

This article is a narrative review summarizing findings from multiple fMRI and MRS studies rather than reporting original, empirical fMRI data. It does not present a within-participant drug-cue>control contrast or any original group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical maps; instead it cites and summarizes other primary studies. Therefore it does not meet the requirement for original task-evoked whole-brain fMRI results and is a non-empirical paper, meeting exclusion criterion E6.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with an event-related within-subject contrast (smoking cue > neutral cue). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked analyses (FDR-corrected) with coordinates/figures and provides participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8/FSL). Thus it satisfies all required inclusion criteria and does not violate exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI cue-reactivity study of alcohol, but the cue modality is gustatory (taste) rather than visual. The meta-analysis specifically requires studies using visual stimuli (I3). Although the study meets other requirements (within-participant alcohol>control contrast, whole-brain group-level voxelwise analyses, and reports relevant methods/software), it fails to satisfy I3 (visual stimuli), so it must be excluded.

This study reports task-based fMRI data using visual smoking cues (smoking-related pictures) with within-subject contrasts (smoking vs. neutral) and behavioral measures of craving. The manuscript presents group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (2×2 ANOVA, FWE-corrected results reported) and tables/figures of activations, satisfying the whole-brain reporting requirement. Relevant participant characteristics (sex distribution, age reported in Table 1), cue type (nicotine/smoking), and analytic details are described in the main text with acquisition/preprocessing/analysis details referenced in the Supporting Information (acceptable per I6). The study therefore meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 and does not violate any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject task activation maps).

This is an empirical task-based fMRI study using smoking-related videos (visual drug cues) and matched control videos, with within-subject contrasts of smoking>control implemented at first-level and group-level random-effects whole-brain analyses (FEAT/FLAME1) with TFCE correction. The paper reports whole-brain statistical maps (figures and supplementary tables), participant age and sex, cue type (nicotine), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). Thus it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This empirical fMRI study investigates visual food cue reactivity (natural reward). It reports within-subject contrasts of food > neutral cues and conducts group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (exploratory 1-sample t test, FWE-corrected) presented in figures/tables. Participant age and sex, cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5) are all reported. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual reward-related cues (images of a $100 bill vs blurry rectangle) and tests neural responses within-subjects under nicotine vs placebo. The paper reports within-participant contrasts (reward > nonreward), whole-brain voxelwise group analyses with cluster-correction (z>3.1, p<.05), and provides participant age/sex, cue type (monetary reward), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). Results are reported in tables/figures and described in text. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 for the meta-analysis.

This study conducted an fMRI cue-reactivity paradigm using visual alcohol cues (90 pictures of alcoholic and neutral beverages), implemented within-subject contrasts (alcoholic vs non-alcoholic beverages), and performed group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses using AFNI (3dMVM) with multiple-comparison correction (3dClustSim). Participant age and sex (female, 21–65) are reported and scanner field strengths (3T) are specified. Whole-brain significant clusters for alcohol cue responses (including anterior cingulate) are reported. These elements satisfy all inclusion criteria for inclusion in the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine (cigarette) cue-reactivity that used visual presentation (participants held and viewed a cigarette via live video) and included a within-participant cigarette vs control (roll of tape) contrast. Analyses were whole-brain, group-level voxelwise maps with cluster-correction and results reported in tables of coordinates and figures. The manuscript reports participant age and sex (male-only sample, mean age reported), cue type (nicotine), MRI field strength (3T Siemens Allegra), and processing software (AFNI, FSL, etc.). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject task activation maps).

This is a task-based BOLD fMRI study that examines neural cue-reactivity to visual food stimuli (natural reward). It includes within-participant contrasts (all food > non-food and high-calorie > low-calorie), reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (FWE-corrected) with detailed results, and provides participant demographics (age, sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3.0 T), and processing software (SPM8). The reported whole-brain statistical maps and corrected cluster results fulfil the meta-analysis requirements. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task maps, or non-empirical) apply.

This is an fMRI study of smoking cue reactivity using visual stimuli and reports within-subject contrasts (smoking cue > control cue). However, all voxel-wise analyses were conducted within an a priori ROI mask rather than as unmasked, group-level whole-brain task activation maps. Inclusion criterion I5 requires at least one group-level, whole-brain (unmasked) voxelwise task-evoked statistical map; that requirement is not met. Therefore the study must be excluded under E3 (reports only ROI-masked analyses with no whole-brain results). Other inclusion criteria met: I3, I4, I6, and I7.

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual food cues (popcorn and Smarties) to elicit natural reward-related cue-reactivity. The paper reports within-subject contrasts comparing food-outcome cues versus a no-outcome control cue, with group-level voxelwise statistical results reported as coordinates and images (permutation-based whole-brain/randomise analyses and supplementary whole-brain results). Participant age/sex, cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL) are all reported. The study therefore meets all inclusion criteria for the review and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes whole-brain task activation maps).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts (food > nonfood) across sleep conditions. Group-level, voxelwise analyses were conducted (GLM, second-level analyses) and at least one whole-brain, FWE-corrected result is reported (parametric modulation and whole-brain regression showing fusiform gyrus), satisfying the requirement for whole-brain task-evoked maps. The paper reports participant age and sex (healthy young adult males), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8). Results are reported with coordinates and figures. No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, not between-group-only, and not non-empirical). Therefore the study meets all listed inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity to natural rewards using visual stimuli.

This is a clinical behavioral study measuring heroin craving with a visual analogue scale in methadone maintenance patients. It does not include any fMRI data, visual cue-reactivity paradigms using visual stimuli in an fMRI context, within-participant fMRI contrasts (drug cues > control), or group-level whole-brain voxelwise activation maps. Therefore it fails to meet inclusion criteria I3–I7 and lacks original fMRI data required for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food images) and within-subject manipulations of sleep with task contrasts comparing food cue types (unhealthy vs healthy; go vs no-go). The paper reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (3dMVM) with significant clusters and peak MNI coordinates in tables/figures, satisfying whole-brain reporting. Required methodological details are present (participant ages/sex distribution, cue type = food, 3T Siemens scanner, processing software AFNI/ANTs). Therefore all inclusion criteria for natural reward visual cue-reactivity are met and no exclusion criteria are triggered.

Full text was not accessible and available study information (title and journal entry) indicates this is a tDCS modulation study in drug addiction rather than an fMRI cue-reactivity experiment using visual stimuli. I cannot verify critical inclusion criteria: whether the study is an fMRI task of drug or natural reward-related visual cue-reactivity (I3), whether it reports within-participant contrasts of cues>control (I4), or whether it reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise task-evoked maps (I5/I6). Because original fMRI task activation data cannot be confirmed from the supplied information, the study does not meet the review’s required inclusion criteria.

This study is a clinical/behavioral factor analysis of deep phenotyping in alcohol use disorder and does not include any fMRI data or task-based visual cue-reactivity paradigm. It does not report within-participant contrasts of drug/natural reward cues > control stimuli, nor any group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation maps, MRI scanner parameters, or processing software. Therefore it fails to meet all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis (I3–I7). No exclusion criteria E3–E5 (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only without task effect) specifically apply, so exclusion is based on failure to meet inclusion criteria.

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-participant contrasts of hedonic food images > nonfood control images. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed (random-effects, voxel-wise p<0.01, cluster-level FDR q<0.05) and coordinates/tables/figures are reported. Relevant methodological details are provided (participant age/sex, cue type = food, 3.0T scanner, SPM8 processing). The study therefore meets all required inclusion criteria for a meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

Although this is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli with within-participant smoking>neutral contrasts and reports participant age/sex, scanner (3T) and processing software (FSL), the paper does not report a group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map for the current dataset. Analyses are focused on an a priori PCC ROI and a voxelwise analysis restricted to the PCC; no unmasked whole-brain task activation map is provided. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I5 and meets exclusion criterion E3.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual drug-related cues (pictures) with a within-subject contrast of drug-related > neutral pictures. The paper reports group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked maps (main effect thresholded at p<.05 FWE; whole-brain exploratory results reported), includes figures/tables/supplementary materials, and provides participant age and sex (male adolescents), cue type (stimulants/cocaine/methamphetamine), MRI scanner field strength (1.5T mobile Siemens Avanto), and processing software (SPM12). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject task activation maps).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual smoking cues (BEGIN/END and other smoking pictures) and matched neutral controls. The authors report within-subject contrasts of smoking versus control stimuli (e.g., S2-T2 BEGIN; S4-T4 END) and second-level, group-level voxelwise analyses conducted in SPM5 (1.5 T scanner) with whole-brain results reported (tables/figures/contrast maps described). Participant age/sex counts, cue type (nicotine/smoking), scanner field strength, and processing software are provided. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for task-evoked, whole-brain fMRI cue-reactivity using visual stimuli.

This is an empirical fMRI study of cigarette (nicotine) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (video clips). The paper reports within-participant contrasts of smoking vs. non-smoking cues, conducts group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (FSL/FLAME1) with thresholded statistical maps, and provides participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software. These elements satisfy all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual stimuli (high-calorie food images) to probe natural reward-related cue-reactivity in a within-subject design (fructose vs. glucose crossover). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (drink x cue interaction; significant visual cortex cluster, Z>2.3, p<0.05 corrected) in addition to ROI results, and provides participant age/sex, MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL). Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an fMRI study of nicotine (smoking) cue reactivity using standardized visual stimuli and an event‐related cue exposure task. The paper reports within-participant contrasts (smoking vs neutral cues), and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses (ANOVA and regressions) with FDR-corrected statistical maps and reported coordinates/tables/figures. Participant information (age, sex—male-only sample), cue type (nicotine), MRI scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM8, FSL) are reported. The study does not rely solely on ROI or connectivity-only analyses. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural/appetitive cues (happy faces) and neutral control (calm faces). It contains within-subject contrasts of happy vs. calm (reward-related cue > control) and reports ventral striatal sensitivity to happy faces. Group-level, voxelwise whole-brain analyses were performed (e.g., nogo > go; right IFG found at whole-brain corrected threshold) and peak coordinates/clusters are reported. The paper provides required methodological/participant details: participant ages and sex distribution, cue type (happy/calm faces), MRI field strength (3.0T), and processing software (AFNI).
