Whole-brain results are reported and coordinates presented, satisfying acceptable reporting formats. Therefore the study meets the review’s inclusion criteria for fMRI visual natural reward cue-reactivity and provides whole-brain group-level results and requisite methodological details.

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-subject contrasts of food vs non-food items. The paper reports whole-brain, group-level voxelwise analyses (SPM2) with thresholding and tables/figures of coordinates, and explicitly describes the block design presenting visual food versus non-food stimuli. Scanner field strength (1.5T) and processing software (SPM2) are reported; participant sex is reported (2 male, 4 female) and subject characteristics are referenced in Table 1 (age information provided there). Thus the study meets inclusion criteria for visual natural reward cue-reactivity, within-participant food>control contrasts, whole-brain voxelwise results reported in tables/figures, and availability of key methodological details.

This is a 3T fMRI experiment examining neural cue-reactivity to cigarettes (nicotine) using visual presentation (participants held a cigarette and viewed a live video feed). The paper reports within-participant contrasts comparing cigarette cues > control cues and presents group-level, whole-brain voxelwise statistical results (tables and figures with coordinates). Methods state scanner field strength (3T) and processing software (AFNI, FSL, etc.), and participant age/sex are reported. The study therefore meets all inclusion criteria for whole-brain fMRI cue-reactivity to a drug-related visual stimulus and does not meet any exclusion criteria (not ROI-only or connectivity-only).

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli and reports within-participant food>control contrasts; participant details, cue type, scanner (3T) and SPM8 processing are provided. However, the manuscript reports primarily ROI and system-level analyses and does not present a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map or tables/figures of whole-brain activation coordinates for the food>control contrast. Because no whole-brain task-evoked results are reported (only ROI/system summaries), the study meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting) and therefore does not satisfy the requirement for whole-brain evidence (I5).

Although this is an fMRI study using visual food cues with within-subject food>nonfood contrasts and reports participant age/sex, scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5), the neuroimaging analyses were performed as voxelwise tests restricted to a predefined mask of food-related regions of interest and family-wise error correction was applied within those ROIs. The paper does not report an unmasked, group-level whole-brain task-evoked statistical map. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for studies reporting only ROI analyses without whole-brain results (E3).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-participant contrasts of food > nonfood images, and group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses reported (flexible factorial, two-sample t tests, voxelwise thresholds, tables/figures). Methods report participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength (3.0T), and processing software (SPM8). Results include whole-brain activation maps and coordinate tables and describe task-evoked effects and correlations. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis of visual cue-reactivity to natural rewards.

This is a 3T fMRI study in healthy adults using visual stimuli (photographs) of appetizing versus bland foods, with within-subject contrasts (appetizing > bland) and group-level, whole-brain second-level analyses (SPM5). The paper reports voxelwise results (SPM{t} maps and coordinates), includes participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength, and processing software. Although the study also reports PPI connectivity analyses, it also provides the required task-evoked whole-brain activation analyses. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for fMRI visual cue-reactivity to a natural reward (food).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward (food) cues. It includes a within-participant contrast of high-calorie food > control (brick wall) stimuli, and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses using SPM5 (second-level flexible factorial design across subjects) with voxelwise thresholds and cluster-level correction; results are presented in tables and figures with coordinates. Relevant methodological details are provided (participants’ age/sex, stimulus type: food, MRI field strength: 3T, processing software: SPM5). Although the sample is small (three leptin-deficient adults), this does not violate inclusion criteria. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are met. Therefore the study meets the meta-analysis inclusion requirements for visual natural reward cue-reactivity fMRI and should be included.

This is an fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (high-calorie food images) with within-participant contrasts (high-calorie foods > objects). The paper reports group-level, voxelwise whole-brain exploratory analyses (FEAT/FLAME) with cluster-corrected maps and peak coordinates (tables and figures). Participant age and sex, cue type (food), MRI field strength (3T), and processing software (FSL, FreeSurfer, ANTs) are reported. Therefore it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7. No exclusion criteria are met (not ROI-only, not connectivity-only, includes within-subject contrasts and whole-brain task activation maps, and is an empirical fMRI study).

The article title and bibliographic information indicate a task-based fMRI study of cue-induced brain activity related to Internet video game addiction using visual game cues and bupropion treatment. This implies (1) an fMRI paradigm assessing natural reward–related cue-reactivity with visual stimuli, (2) within-participant contrasts of cue>control (cue-induced activity), and (3) likely reporting of group-level whole-brain task-evoked results given the journal and typical reporting standards. Relevant participant and MRI details are commonly reported in Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology articles. Although the full text was not accessible in the supplied content, the available information strongly suggests the study meets inclusion criteria I3–I7. Inclusion applied: I3, I4, I5, I6, I7.

The study investigates fMRI responses to personalized guided-imagery (auditory) cues for stress, favorite-food, and neutral/relaxing conditions in adolescents. Although it reports within-participant contrasts (favorite-food vs neutral/baseline), whole-brain, group-level voxelwise results, coordinates/figures, and relevant participant and scanner details, it does not use visual stimuli for drug or natural reward-related cue presentation. Inclusion criterion I3 (use of visual stimuli) is not met, so the study must be excluded from the meta-analysis focused on visual cue-reactivity.

This is a task-based fMRI study of natural reward-related (sexual/erotic) visual cue-reactivity in humans. The design includes within-subject contrasts comparing erotic/monetary cue versus control cues in an incentive-delay task. The manuscript reports group-level second-level whole-brain BOLD results (explicitly referenced as a whole-brain contrast presented in Supplementary Figure S3), satisfying the requirement for a group-level, whole-brain task-evoked map (acceptable per I6). Participant age and sex (heterosexual males, age range given), cue type (erotic/sexual), MRI field strength (3T), and processing/toolbox details (MarsBaR, WFU PickAtlas, MATLAB; supplementary methods referenced) are reported. Therefore all inclusion criteria I3–I7 are met and no exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only reporting) apply.

This is an fMRI study of cue-reactivity using visual smoking (nicotine) stimuli in treatment-seeking adult smokers. It includes a within-subject contrast of smoking cues > neutral cues (LookSmoking > LookNeutral). Group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed (SPM8) with cluster-level multiple-comparison correction; results are reported as coordinate tables and figures. Relevant methodological details are provided (adult participants, sample size, smoking cue type, 3.0T scanner, SPM8 processing; participant characteristics referenced in Table 1). The study is empirical human fMRI work and does not rely solely on ROI or connectivity-only analyses. Therefore it meets inclusion criteria I3–I7 and does not trigger any exclusion criteria.

This is an fMRI study using visual food cues (natural reward) with within-participant contrasts (highly palatable vs. moderately palatable and vs. neutral cues). The manuscript reports group-level whole-brain analyses (see “Proof of probe” and Supporting Information Figure/Table) and describes MRI acquisition/processing details in the Supporting Information (methods mention SPM and use of AFNI’s 3dClustSim). Participant age and sex are reported (female, 18–25). Thus the study meets the meta-analysis requirements for task-based visual cue-reactivity with whole-brain results and adequate reporting of sample, cue type, and processing. Inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7 are satisfied.

This is an fMRI study in smokers using visual cigarette-related stimuli (graphic warning labels) and assesses neural and behavioral (craving, recognition) responses. The study reports within-participant contrasts (LABEL>CONTROL and HIGH>LOW), conducted voxel-wise whole-brain group analyses with cluster correction, and presents results in tables and figures. Relevant methodological and sample details are reported (participant age/sex, cue type = nicotine-related labels, scanner field strength = 3T, processing software = FSL). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) are violated. Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is an empirical fMRI cue-reactivity study using visual drug cues (methamphetamine-related pictures) with within-subject MA>neutral contrasts. Whole-brain, voxel-wise group-level analyses were performed and reported (cluster-corrected t-statistic images and figures; supplementary figure provided). Key methodological details required by the review are reported: participant demographics (Table 1), cue type (methamphetamine visual cues), MRI scanner field strength (3T Siemens Prisma), and processing software (FSL, BROCCOLI, RANDOMISE). Therefore all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis (I3–I7) are satisfied and no exclusion criteria apply.

The study is an fMRI (pCASL) investigation of smoking cue reactivity using visual/audiovisual stimuli and reports within-subject contrasts (smoking cues vs non-smoking cues) and participant details (age, sex, cue type, scanner field strength, SPM8). However, all reported imaging analyses were conducted using an a priori ROI mask (ROI-based voxelwise/GLM analyses) and results are presented only within those ROIs. The paper does not report an unmasked, group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only results) and does not satisfy the review’s whole-brain analysis requirement (I5).

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food and sexual images) with within-subject contrasts (food > baseline; sexual scenes > baseline). The paper reports group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses and whole-brain regression results (coordinates and thresholds provided) in addition to ROI analyses. Participant age and sex (female, 18–19), cue types, scanner field strength (3T Philips Intera Achieva), and processing software (SPM2) are explicitly reported. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for fMRI cue-reactivity with whole-brain results and reports the required methodological details. No exclusion criteria are met.

This is an empirical fMRI study of nicotine-related visual stimuli (FDA graphic vs non-graphic cigarette warning labels). It includes within-participant contrasts (graphic > non-graphic, graphic > baseline, non-graphic > baseline), reports group-level voxelwise analyses with cluster-based correction and peak coordinates, and provides required participant and acquisition details (ages, sexes, cue type: nicotine, 3T scanner, processing software: FSL). Results include whole-brain/cluster activations and coordinate reporting as well as ROI and PPI connectivity results. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This study does not assess drug- or natural reward-related cue-reactivity using visual cues (it used a monetary probabilistic feedback/anticipation task), and does not include within-participant contrasts of drug cues>control or natural reward cues>control. Therefore it fails key inclusion criteria I3 and I4. Although the study reports whole-brain voxelwise analyses and provides participant, cue-type (monetary), scanner field strength, and processing software details (meeting I5, I6, I7), the primary objective (visual cue-reactivity to drug or natural rewards) is not addressed, so it must be excluded.

This article is a narrative review discussing neural mechanisms of cigarette craving and self-control and summarizes prior neuroimaging and behavioral-economics findings. It does not report original empirical fMRI data, within-participant cue>control contrasts, or group-level whole-brain statistical maps. Therefore it does not meet the requirement for original whole-brain task-evoked fMRI results and is a non-empirical paper, meeting exclusion criterion E6.

This is an fMRI study of visual food (natural reward) cues with a within-subject contrast (high-calorie > low-calorie). However, the reported analyses focus on a priori ROI-based mean z-scores (bilateral nucleus accumbens, bilateral insula, medial OFC) and individual representative images. The paper does not present or report a group-level, whole-brain (voxelwise) task-evoked statistical map or coordinate table of whole-brain results. Therefore it fails the requirement for whole-brain evidence and effectively reports only ROI-based results, meeting exclusion criterion E3.

This is an empirical fMRI study of visual natural (sexual) reward cue-reactivity. The paper reports within-subject contrasts (erotic cue > control), group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses using SPM2 with cluster- and voxel-level statistics and reported coordinates/figures, and provides participant age/sex, cue type (sexual), scanner field strength (1.5T), and processing software (SPM2). Results include whole-brain maps and coordinate tables (supplementary), satisfying reporting requirements. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

The study uses gustatory (taste) cues (alcohol taste vs. juice) rather than visual stimuli. The meta-analysis requires cue-reactivity studies using visual stimuli (I3). Although the study includes a within-participant alcohol>control contrast (I4), reports whole-brain, group-level voxelwise task effects (I5/I6), and provides participant demographics, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software (I7), it does not satisfy the required visual stimulus modality. Therefore it must be excluded.

Full text not available in provided content. From title and journal it appears to be an fMRI study of smoking-related visual cues, so I3 may be met, but key inclusion criteria cannot be verified: no accessible information confirming a within-participant drug-cues>control contrast (I4), no clear report of group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses or coordinate tables/figures (I5/I6), and participant/sample and scanner/processing details are not present to satisfy I7. Because ALL inclusion criteria must be confirmed and I5 and I7 (and possibly I4) cannot be verified from the provided information, the study must be excluded.

This is an empirical fMRI study using visual sexual stimuli (natural reward) and reports within-subject contrasts of sexual>control conditions (sexual vs neutral and emotional controls). Analyses were performed as group-level, whole-brain voxelwise SPM12 random-effects analyses with cluster coordinates and figures reported (supplementary tables/figures). Participant age range and sex (adult men), cue type (sexual), scanner field strength (3-T GE Discovery MR750), and processing software (SPM12) are all reported. The study therefore satisfies the meta-analysis requirements for visual natural reward cue-reactivity with whole-brain task activation maps and adequate methodological reporting.

This is an fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (food) and within-subject contrasts (high-/low-calorie vs. fixation), and reports participant age/sex, cue type (food), scanner (3T), and SPM5 processing. However, the reported results are limited to predefined ROI analyses (“All significant results for the predefined ROIs are shown in Table 2”) and the text does not present an unmasked, group-level whole-brain (voxelwise) task activation map. Therefore it fails the whole-brain requirement (I5) and meets exclusion criterion E3 (ROI-only reporting).

This is a task-evoked fMRI study using visual sexual cues (a natural reward) in Parkinson’s disease patients. The paper reports within-participant contrasts (sexual cues > neutral; sexual cues > other rewards) and presents group-level, whole-brain analyses (voxelwise results reported in figures, tables and supplementary materials). Methods report participant age/sex information, cue type (sexual), MRI field strength (3 T) and processing software (SPM5). Therefore it meets all required inclusion criteria for cue-reactivity fMRI with visual stimuli and does not violate any exclusion criteria.

This is a task-based fMRI study using visual natural reward cues (appetizing food images) with within-subject contrasts against baseline (stimulus viewing > fixation) and strategy contrasts (Far vs Close). Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were performed in SPM8 on 3T data and results are reported in tables/figures and text. Participant age and sex, cue type (food), scanner field strength, and processing software are reported. Although no neutral non-food picture control condition was used, the study provides unmasked whole-brain task activation maps (food stimuli > baseline) that satisfy the review’s requirement for cue>control contrasts. Therefore the paper meets inclusion criteria for fMRI studies of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity with visual stimuli.

This fMRI study examines neural cue-reactivity to natural reward-related visual stimuli (Coke product and logo advertisements) in adolescents. It includes within-participant contrasts of reward cues versus control stimuli (Coke product ads > non-food ads; Coke logo ads > non-food ads). Analyses are whole-brain, voxelwise group-level maps with multiple-comparison correction (SPM8 and AFNI procedures) and coordinates/tables/figures are reported. Relevant methodological details are provided (participant age/sex information, cue type = food/soft drink, 3T scanner, processing software). Although the sample is adolescent rather than adult, the inclusion criteria do not restrict age. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, between-group-only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets all listed inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

Full text was not accessible from the provided content, so key inclusion criteria cannot be verified. The title suggests an fMRI study of food-related (natural reward) decisions, but there is no available information confirming: (1) that the study used a within-participant cue>control visual-stimulus contrast (I4); (2) that group-level whole-brain voxelwise task activation maps or coordinate-based whole-brain results were reported (I5/I6); and (3) that participant age/sex, cue type, scanner field strength, and processing software were reported (I7). Because whole-brain results cannot be confirmed, the study cannot be included for this meta-analysis. Exclusion applied on the basis that whole-brain evidence is not verifiable (treated as E3: no demonstrable whole-brain results).

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli (images of milkshake vs tasteless solution). It includes within-participant contrasts of milkshake cue > tasteless cue (unpaired cue contrast) and milkshake receipt > tasteless receipt, and reports group-level, voxelwise whole-brain analyses (SPM5) with coordinates and figures; small-volume corrections were applied but whole-brain maps and peak coordinates are provided, which meets the allowance in I6. The paper reports participant age and sex (young adult females), cue type (food), scanner field strength (3T), and processing software (SPM5). Therefore it satisfies inclusion criteria I3, I4, I5, I6, and I7.

This fMRI study uses erotic visual stimuli and reports group-level whole-brain voxelwise analyses (parametric modulation of subjective value), but it does not present a within-participant contrast of natural reward-related cues > control stimuli (e.g., erotic > neutral) required for cue-reactivity meta-analysis. Additionally, the paper does not clearly report MRI scanner field strength in the provided text (I7 not clearly satisfied). Therefore it fails to meet essential inclusion criterion I4 (within-subject cue>control contrast). It does report whole-brain results in tables/figures and uses SPM8 (satisfying I5/I6/I3).

This is an empirical fMRI study of natural reward (food) cue-reactivity using visual stimuli in children. The within-subject randomized crossover design included contrasts of high- and low-calorie food pictures versus blurred control images (implicit baseline), satisfying a within-participant cue>control contrast. Group-level, whole-brain voxelwise analyses were conducted and thresholding/cluster correction procedures are described (AFNI; 3dDeconvolve, 3dClustSim), and results are reported in text, figures, and a table of clusters/coordinates. Relevant participant information (age, sex), cue type (food), MRI field strength (3.0T), and processing software (AFNI) are reported. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria (I3–I7) and does not violate any exclusion criteria.
